tEn: Naval Ravikant on the Secret to the All-In Podcast's Success
tZh: Naval Ravikant 揭秘 All-In 播客成功秘诀
sEn: Naval Ravikant explains why the All-In podcast is the most fun he's ever recorded, citing the unique dynamic of four intelligent hosts who genuinely enjoy each other's company.
sZh: Naval Ravikant 解释为何 All-In 播客是他录过最有趣的节目，指出四位聪明主持人之间独特的化学反应和真诚的互动是关键。

consensus.en: AI is a huge opportunity, not just a safety risk; the US must lead.
consensus.zh: AI 是巨大机遇，而不仅是安全风险；美国必须领先。

consensus.en: Overregulation of AI could kill innovation; a light-touch approach is better.
consensus.zh: 过度监管 AI 可能扼杀创新；轻触式方法更佳。

consensus.en: AI will create more jobs than it destroys, following historical tech trends.
consensus.zh: AI 创造的就业机会将多于其摧毁的，符合历史技术趋势。

consensus.en: Open-source AI is crucial to prevent centralization of power in few hands.
consensus.zh: 开源 AI 对于防止权力集中在少数人手中至关重要。

consensus.en: Sleep is the single most important factor for health and longevity.
consensus.zh: 睡眠是健康和长寿的最重要单一因素。

contrarian.en: AI doomerism is overblown, often pushed by companies with vested interests.
contrarian.zh: AI 末日论被夸大，常由有既得利益的公司推动。

contrarian.en: Taking children seriously—giving them freedom like adults—leads to happier kids.
contrarian.zh: 认真对待孩子——像对待成年人一样给予自由——会带来更快乐的孩子。

contrarian.en: Tariffs may be necessary to protect strategic industries and the middle class.
contrarian.zh: 关税可能是保护战略产业和中产阶级所必需的。

contrarian.en: AI models trained on open web data should be open source as a trade-off.
contrarian.zh: 基于开放网络数据训练的 AI 模型应作为交换开源。

contrarian.en: The New York Times may win its AI copyright case, forcing licensing payments.
contrarian.zh: 纽约时报可能赢得 AI 版权案，迫使支付许可费。

turns.en: Great job, Naval. You rocked it. Maybe I should have said this on air, but that was literally the most fun podcast I've ever recorded.
turns.zh: 干得好，Naval。你太棒了。也许我应该在节目里说，但那真的是我录过的最有趣的播客。

turns.en: Oh, that's on air? Cut that in. Yeah, put it in the show.
turns.zh: 哦，这是播出的吗？剪进去。对，放到节目里。

turns.en: I had my theory on why you were number one, but now I have the realization. What's the actual reason? You know us for a long time.
turns.zh: 我本来有个理论解释你为什么是第一，但现在我明白了。真正的原因是什么？你认识我们很久了。

turns.en: Yeah, what was your theory? What's the reality?
turns.zh: 是啊，你的理论是什么？现实又是什么？

turns.en: My theory was that my problem with going on podcasts is usually the person I'm talking to is not that interesting. They're just asking the same questions, they're dialing it in, and they're not that interested. It's not like we're having a peer-level actual conversation. So that's why I wanted to do Airchat and Clubhouse and things like that, because you can actually have a conversation.
turns.zh: 我的理论是，我上播客的问题通常是和我聊天的人没那么有趣。他们只是问同样的问题，敷衍了事，而且并不真的感兴趣。我们并不是在进行同级别的真正对话。所以这就是为什么我想做Airchat和Clubhouse这类东西，因为你可以真正地对话。

turns.en: I see. Right. And what you guys have very uniquely is four people, of whom at least three are intelligent. I'm kidding. How could you say that? SX isn't even here to hear you say that. That is so cold. Best right. These three are intelligent, and all of you get along, and you can have an ongoing conversation. That's a very high hit rate. Normally in a podcast you only get one interesting person, and now you've got three, maybe four. Right? Okay, so that to me was why. And by the way, you're talking to number four. We don't know, she remains mysterious forever. Of the four, right? The problem is like, if you get people together to talk, two is a good conversation, three possibly four is the max. That's why at a dinner table in a restaurant, four top, right? You don't do five or six because then it splits into multiple conversations. So you had four people who were capable of talking, right? I thought that was the secret, but there's another secret. The other secret is you guys are having fun. You're talking over each other, you're making fun of each other, you're actually having fun. So that's why I'm saying this is the most fun podcast I've ever been on. And that's why you'll be welcome back anytime.
turns.zh: 我明白了。对。而你们非常独特的一点是，你们有四个人，其中至少三个是聪明的。我开玩笑的。你怎么能这么说？SX都不在这儿听你说。太刻薄了。最好的。这三个很聪明，而且你们都相处得很好，可以进行持续的对话。这成功率非常高。通常在一个播客里你只有一个有趣的人，而现在你有三个，也许四个。对吧？所以对我来说这就是原因。顺便说一句，你正在和第四个人说话。我们不知道，她永远神秘。四个人里，对吧？问题是，如果你把人们聚在一起聊天，两个人是好的对话，三个人可能四个是上限。这就是为什么在餐厅的餐桌上，四人桌，对吧？你不会安排五个人或六个人，因为那样对话就会分裂成多个。所以你们有四个能聊天的人，对吧？我以为这就是秘密，但还有另一个秘密。另一个秘密是你们玩得很开心。你们互相插话，互相取笑，你们真的在享受。所以这就是为什么我说这是我参加过的最有趣的播客。这也是为什么你随时欢迎回来。

turns.en: No, all welcome back. Yes, absolutely. In three smart guys. I can't even believe you'd say that about Sacks, he's not even here to defend himself. Sorry, David. Let your winners ride. We open sources to the fans and they've just gone crazy with it. All right, everybody welcome back to the number one podcast in the world. We're really excited today. Back again, your sultan of science, David Friedberg. What do you got going on there, Friedberg? What's in the background?
turns.zh: 不，都欢迎回来。是的，当然。三个聪明人。我简直不敢相信你会这么说Sacks，他甚至不在场为自己辩护。抱歉，David。让赢家继续。我们向粉丝开源，他们简直疯了。好了，欢迎大家回到世界第一播客。今天非常兴奋。再次回来，你的科学苏丹，David Friedberg。你在那儿干什么呢，Friedberg？背景里是什么？

turns.en: I used to play a lot of a game called SimEarth on my Macintosh LC way back in the day. That tracks. Yeah, that tracks. And of course with us again, your chairman. What games did you play growing up, J? Actually, I'm kind of curious, did you ever play video games?
turns.zh: 我以前在我的Macintosh LC上玩过很多叫《模拟地球》的游戏，很久以前了。说得通。是啊，说得通。当然还有我们的主席。你小时候玩什么游戏，J？其实我有点好奇，你玩过电子游戏吗？

turns.en: Let's say Andrea Allison, uh, Susan. I mean, it was like a lot of cute girls. I was out dating girls, Friedberg. Yeah, I was not on my Apple II playing Civilization. Let me find one of those pictures of who... Whoa, don't get me in trouble, man. The 80s were good to me in Brooklyn. Rejection the video game. Yes, you have three lives: rejected, rejected, rejected. It's a numbers game, Chamath, as you know, as you well know. It is a numbers game. Yeah. Nick, go ahead, pull up Rico Suave here. Oh no, what is this one? Instead of playing video games, here I am. No, the 80s. That's fat, that's fat. J, you want to get Nick to help out your uncle? Yeah, here he is out slaying. Help out your uncle with the VIN. Jock C, you know what he was slinging in there? Snacks, yeah. Pre- and post-asm, right? Correct. And weightlifting, beef jerky. Go find my Leonardo DiCaprio picture, please, and replace my fat jow picture with that. Thank you. Oh God, I was fat, man. Plus 40 lbs is a lot heavier than I... It's no joke, it's no joke. 40 lbs is a lot. There's so many great emo photos of me. I'm proud of you. Thank you, my man. Thank you, my man. If you want a good... Can you get through the intros, please, so we can start? Come on, quick. How you doing, brother? How you doing, chairman, dictator? You good? All right, all right. We're really excited today. Today, for the first time on the All-In podcast, the Iron Fist of AngelList, the Zen master of the early stage. He has such a way with words. He's the sorcerer of nerds. Please welcome my guy, Namaste Naval. How you doing?
turns.zh: 比如说Andrea Allison，呃，Susan。我的意思是，有很多可爱的女孩。我出去约会女孩，Friedberg。是啊，我没有在我的Apple II上玩《文明》。让我找一张那些照片……哇，别给我惹麻烦，伙计。80年代在布鲁克林对我很好。《拒绝》电子游戏。是的，你有三条命：被拒绝，被拒绝，被拒绝。这是个数字游戏，Chamath，你知道，你很清楚。这是个数字游戏。是啊。Nick，来吧，把Rico Suave调出来。哦不，这是什么？不是玩电子游戏，我在这儿。不，80年代。那很胖，那很胖。J，你想让Nick帮你叔叔吗？是啊，他在这儿大杀四方。帮你叔叔弄VIN。Jock C，你知道他在那儿卖什么吗？零食，是啊。前后都卖，对吧？正确。还有举重，牛肉干。请找到我的莱昂纳多·迪卡普里奥照片，把我那张胖下巴的照片换掉。谢谢。哦天哪，我那时候很胖，伙计。多了40磅比我重多了……这不是开玩笑，不是开玩笑。40磅很多。我有很多很棒的emo照片。我为你骄傲。谢谢你，老兄。谢谢你，老兄。如果你想要好的……你能把介绍做完吗，这样我们就可以开始了？快点。你好吗，兄弟？你好吗，主席，独裁者？你还好吗？好了，好了。我们今天非常兴奋。今天，在All-In播客上首次，AngelList的铁拳，早期的禅宗大师。他真有口才。他是书呆子的巫师。请欢迎我的哥们，Namaste Naval。你好吗？

turns.en: The intros are back. That is the best intro I've ever gotten. I didn't think you could do that. That was amazing. That's your superpower right there. Lock it in. Quit venture capital. Just do that. Absolutely. That's actually, you know what? Interestingly, number one podcast in the world, like someone said. I mean, that's what I'm manifesting. It's getting close. We've been in the top 10. So I mean, the weekends are good for All-In. This one will hit number one. This one will go viral. I think it could. If you have some really great pithy insights, we might go right to the top. I just got to do a séance and it'll go viral. Oh no, oh no. Are you gonna send us your heart? My heart goes out to you. My heart... I end here at the heart. I don't send it out. I keep it right here. I put both hands on the heart and I hold it nice and steady. I hold it in. It's sending out to you, but just not explicitly.
turns.zh: 介绍回来了。这是我得到过的最好的介绍。我没想到你能做到。太棒了。那就是你的超能力。锁定它。放弃风险投资。就做这个。当然。实际上，你知道吗？有趣的是，世界第一播客，就像有人说的。我的意思是，那是我在显化的。它越来越近了。我们一直在前十名。所以我的意思是，周末对All-In来说很好。这一期会达到第一。这一期会病毒式传播。我觉得可以。如果你有一些非常精辟的见解，我们可能会直接登顶。我只需要做个降神会，它就会病毒式传播。哦不，哦不。你会把你的心送给我们吗？我的心与你同在。我的心……我在这儿以心结束。我不把它送出去。我把它留在这里。我把双手放在心上，稳稳地握住。我把它收住。它在向你发送，只是不明确。

turns.en: All right, for those of you who don't know, Naval was an entrepreneur. He kicked a bit of ass, he got his ass kicked, and then he started Venture Hacks and he started emailing folks and saying, you know, 20, 15 years ago, maybe 15, here are some deals in Silicon Valley. He went around, he started writing 50k, 100k checks. He hit a bunch of home runs and he turned Venture Hacks into AngelList. And then he has invested in a ton of great startups. Maybe give us some of the greatest hits there.
turns.zh: 好了，对于那些不认识的人，Naval曾经是个企业家。他干得不错，也被揍过，然后他创办了Venture Hacks，开始给人们发邮件说，你知道，20、15年前，也许是15年，硅谷有一些交易。他到处跑，开始写5万、10万美元的支票。他打出了一堆本垒打，把Venture Hacks变成了AngelList。然后他投资了大量优秀的初创公司。也许给我们一些最成功的案例。

turns.en: Dev, yeah. Twitter, Uber, Notion, bunch of others. Postmates, you, me. A lot of unicorns, bunch of up-and-coming. I mean, it's actually a lot of deals at this point. But honestly, I'm not necessarily proud of being an investor. Investor to me is a side job, it's a hobby. So I do startups.
turns.zh: Dev，是啊。Twitter、Uber、Notion，还有很多其他的。Postmates，你，我。很多独角兽，很多后起之秀。我的意思是，实际上现在有很多交易。但老实说，我并不一定为成为投资者而自豪。投资者对我来说是副业，是爱好。所以我做初创公司。

turns.en: How do you define yourself?
turns.zh: 你怎么定义自己？

turns.en: I don't. I mean, I guess these days I would say more like building things. You know, every so-called career is an evolution, right? And all of you guys are independent and you kind of do what you're most interested in, right? That's the point of making money, so you can just do what you want. So these days I'm really into building and crafting products. So I built one recently called Airchat. It kind of didn't work. I'm still proud of what I built and got to work with an incredible team. And now I'm building a new product, and this time I'm going into hardware. And I'm just building something that I really want. I'm not doing it all yourself, Naval. Partially I bring investors along. Last time they got their money back. Previous times they've made money. Next time hopefully they'll make a lot of money. It's good to bring your friends along.
turns.zh: 我不定义。我的意思是，我想这些天我会说更像是建造东西。你知道，每个所谓的职业都是一场进化，对吧？你们所有人都是独立的，你们做自己最感兴趣的事，对吧？这就是赚钱的意义，这样你就可以做自己想做的事。所以这些天我真的很喜欢构建和打磨产品。我最近建了一个叫Airchat的东西。它有点没成功。我仍然为我所建造的感到自豪，并且与一个不可思议的团队合作。现在我正在构建一个新产品，这次我要进入硬件领域。我只是在建造一个我真正想要的东西。我不是完全自己做，Naval。部分地我会带投资者一起。上次他们拿回了钱。之前他们赚了钱。下次希望他们会赚很多钱。带朋友一起是好事。

turns.en: I'll be honest, I love that you said you love the product but it didn't work. Not enough people say that.
turns.zh: 老实说，我喜欢你说的你爱那个产品但它没成功。没有足够多的人这么说。

turns.en: Yeah, I know. I built a product that I loved, that I was proud of, but it didn't catch fire. And it was a social product, so it had to catch fire for it to work. So I found the team great homes, they all got paid.
turns.zh: 是啊，我知道。我建了一个我爱的、我自豪的产品，但它没有火起来。而且它是一个社交产品，所以它必须火起来才能成功。所以我为团队找到了好归宿，他们都得到了报酬。

turns.en: The investors that I brought in got their money back and I learned a ton which I'm leveraging into the new thing. But the new thing is much harder. The new thing is hardware and software. And what did you learn building in 2024 and 2025 that you didn't know maybe before then?
turns.zh: 我带来的投资者拿回了他们的钱，我学到了很多，正在把这些经验用到新项目上。但新项目难得多，是硬件和软件的结合。你在2024年和2025年的构建过程中学到了什么以前不知道的东西？

turns.en: The main thing was actually just the craft, the craft of pixel by pixel designing a software product and launching it. I guess the main thing I took away that was a learning was that I really enjoyed building products and that I wanted to build something even harder and something even more real. And I think like a lot of us, I'm inspired by Elon and all the incredible work he's done. So I don't want to build things that are easy. I want to build things that are hard and interesting, and I want to take on more technical risk and less market risk. This is the classic VC learning, right? Which is you want to build something that if people get it, if you can deliver it, you know people will want it, and it's just hard to build, as opposed to you build it and you don't know if they want it. So that's a learning.
turns.zh: 主要是手艺本身，逐像素设计软件产品并发布的手艺。我想我学到的主要是，我真的很喜欢构建产品，并且想做一些更难、更真实的东西。和很多人一样，我也受到埃隆的启发，以及他所做的所有了不起的工作。所以我不想做容易的事，我想做困难而有趣的事，我想承担更多的技术风险，而不是市场风险。这是经典的VC经验，对吧？你想构建的东西是，如果人们理解了，如果你能交付，你知道人们会想要它，只是很难构建；而不是你建好了却不知道人们是否想要。这就是一个教训。

turns.en: Airchat was a lot of fun. For those of you who don't know, it was kind of like a social media network where you could ask a question and then people could respond, and it was like an audio-based Twitter. Would you say that was the best way to describe it? Audio Twitter, asynchronous, AI transcripts and all kinds of AI to make it easier for you, translation. Really good way to try to make podcasting-type conversations more accessible to everybody, because honestly one of the reasons I don't go on podcasts is I don't like being intermediated, so to speak, right? Where you sit there and someone interviews you and then you go back and forth and you go through the same old things. I just want to talk to people. I want peer relationships, kind of like you guys have running here.
turns.zh: Airchat 很有趣。对于那些不知道的人来说，它有点像社交媒体网络，你可以提问，然后人们可以回应，就像基于音频的 Twitter。你会说这是最好的描述方式吗？音频 Twitter，异步，AI 转录和各种 AI 让它更容易，翻译。真正的好方法是尝试让播客类型的对话更容易被所有人接受，因为老实说，我不去播客的原因之一就是我不喜欢被中介，可以这么说，对吧？你坐在那里，有人采访你，然后你来我往，重复同样的事情。我只想和人交谈，我想要对等的关系，就像你们在这里运行的那样。

turns.en: Naval, what happened when you went through that phase? There was a period where it just seemed like something had gone on in your life and you just knew the answers. You were just so grounded. It's not to say that you're not grounded now, but you're less active posting and writing. But there was this period where I think all of us were like, 'Right, what does Naval think? Oh really? Oh okay, that's useful.' I would say it would be like the late teens, the early 20s. Jason, you can correct me if I'm getting the dates wrong. But it's been that moment where like these Naval isms and this sort of philosophy really started. I think people had a tremendous respect for how you were thinking about things. I'm just curious, like what were you going through in that moment?
turns.zh: Naval，你经历那个阶段时发生了什么？有一段时间，你似乎经历了一些事情，然后你就知道了答案。你非常脚踏实地。不是说你现在不脚踏实地，但你发帖和写作没那么活跃了。但有一段时间，我想我们所有人都觉得，‘对，Naval 怎么想？真的吗？哦，好吧，这很有用。’我想那大概是十几岁末，二十岁出头。Jason，如果我说错了日期你可以纠正我。但就是那个时候，这些 Naval 主义思想和哲学真正开始了。我想人们对你思考问题的方式非常尊重。我只是好奇，你当时经历了什么？

turns.en: Yeah, yeah, yeah, that's right. No, very insightful. Yeah, 2010s. I've been on Twitter since 2007 because I was an early investor, but I never really tweeted. I didn't get featured. I had no audience. I was just doing the usual techie guy thing, talking to each other. And then I started AngelList in 2010. The original thing about matching investors to startups didn't scale. It was just an email list that exploded early on but then just didn't scale, so we didn't have a business. And I was trying to figure out the business. And at the same time, I got a letter from the Securities and Exchange Commission saying, 'Oh, you're acting as an unlicensed broker-dealer.' And I'm like, 'What? I'm not making any money. I'm not taking anything. It's just a public service.' But even then, they were coming after me. So I wasn't. And I'd raised a bunch of money from investors, so I was in a very high stress period of my life. Now looking back, it's almost comical that I was stressed over that, but at the time it all felt very real. The weight of everything was on my shoulders: expectations, people, money, regulators. And I eventually went to DC and got the law changed to legalize what we do, which ironically enabled a whole bunch of other things like ICOs and incubator days and demo days. But in that process, I was in a very high stress period of my life, and I just started tweeting whatever I was going through, whatever realizations I was having. It's only in stress that you are forced to grow. And so whatever internal growth I was going through, I just started tweeting it, not thinking much of it. And it was a mix of three things that I kind of always run through: one is I love science, you know, I'm an amateur, love physics, let's just leave it at that. I love reading a lot of philosophy and thinking deeply about it. And I like making money, right? Truth, Love, and Money, that's my joke on my Twitter bio. Those are the three things that I keep coming back to. And so I just started tweeting about all of them. And I think before that, the expectation was that someone like me should just be talking about money, stay in your lane. And people had been playing it very safe. And so I think the combination of the three sort of caught people's attention, because every person thinks about everything. We don't just stay in our lane in real life. We're dealing with our relationships, we're dealing with our relationship with the universe, we're dealing with what we know to be true, with science and how we make decisions and how we figure things out. And we're also dealing with practical everyday material things: how to deal with our spouses or girlfriends or wives or husbands, and how to make money, and how to deal with our children. So I'm just tweeting about everything. I just got interested in everything. I'm tweeting about it. And a lot of it, my best stuff was just notes to self. It's like, 'Hey, don't forget this.' 'How to Get Rich' remember that one? 'How to Get Rich' that thread. Super viral. Yeah, bang, yeah, yeah, yeah. I think that is still the most viral thread ever on Twitter. I like timeless things. I like philosophy. I like things that still apply in the future. I like compound interests, if you will, in ideas. Obviously recently X has become so addictive that we're all checking it every day, and Elon's built the perfect 'for you' page. He's built TikTok for nerds, and we're all on it. But normally I try to ignore the news. Obviously last year things got real, we all had to pay attention to the news. But I just like to tweet timeless things. I don't know, I mean people pay attention sometimes. They like what I write, sometimes they go 'Naval' on me. But yeah, the 'How to Get Rich' thread was a big one.
turns.zh: 是的，是的，是的，没错。不，非常有洞察力。是的，2010年代。我从2007年就开始用Twitter了，因为我是早期投资者，但我从来没有真正发过推文。我没有被推荐，没有粉丝。我只是做通常的技术人员的事情，互相交谈。然后我在2010年创办了AngelList。最初关于匹配投资者和初创公司的想法没有规模化。它只是一个邮件列表，早期爆炸式增长，但后来就没有规模化，所以我们没有业务。我试图弄清楚业务。同时，我收到了美国证券交易委员会的一封信，说‘哦，你在充当无牌经纪交易商。’我说，‘什么？我没赚钱，我没拿任何东西，这只是公共服务。’但即使那样，他们也在追查我。所以我当时压力很大。我还从投资者那里筹集了一大笔钱，所以我处于人生中压力非常大的时期。现在回想起来，我为那件事感到压力几乎有点可笑，但在当时感觉非常真实。一切的重担都压在我肩上：期望、人、钱、监管机构。我最终去了华盛顿，推动了法律修改，使我们做的事情合法化，这讽刺地促成了许多其他事情，比如ICO、孵化器日和演示日。但在那个过程中，我处于人生中压力非常大的时期，我开始发推文，记录我正在经历的事情，我产生的任何领悟。只有在压力下，你才会被迫成长。所以无论我经历了什么样的内心成长，我都开始发推文，没想太多。它混合了三样我经常思考的东西：一是热爱科学，我是个业余爱好者，热爱物理学，就这样吧。我喜欢大量阅读哲学并深入思考。还有我喜欢赚钱，对吧？真理、爱和金钱，这是我Twitter简介上的玩笑。这三样东西是我不断回归的。所以我开始发关于所有这些的推文。我认为在那之前，人们期望像我这样的人应该只谈论金钱，待在自己的领域里。人们一直很谨慎。所以我认为这三者的结合引起了人们的注意，因为每个人都会思考一切。在现实生活中，我们不会只待在自己的领域里。我们处理人际关系，处理我们与宇宙的关系，处理我们认为是真实的东西，处理科学以及我们如何做决定、如何解决问题。我们还处理日常的物质生活：如何与配偶、女友、妻子或丈夫相处，如何赚钱，如何与孩子相处。所以我只是发关于一切的推文。我对一切都感兴趣。我发推文。其中很多最好的东西只是给自己的笔记。比如，‘嘿，别忘了这个。’‘如何致富’还记得那个吗？‘如何致富’那个帖子。超级病毒式传播。是的，砰，是的，是的，是的。我认为那仍然是Twitter上传播最广的帖子。我喜欢永恒的东西。我喜欢哲学。我喜欢在未来仍然适用的东西。我喜欢思想的复利，如果你愿意这么说的话。显然最近X变得如此令人上瘾，我们每天都在看，埃隆建立了完美的‘为你推荐’页面。他为书呆子们建立了TikTok，我们都在上面。但通常我尽量忽略新闻。显然去年事情变得真实，我们都不得不关注新闻。但我只是喜欢发永恒的推文。我不知道，我的意思是人们有时会注意。他们喜欢我写的东西，有时他们会对我‘Naval’一下。但是的，‘如何致富’那个帖子是一个大事件。

transcript.sec: The gap between hype and reality

turns.en: Is it problematic when people now meet you because the hype versus the reality? There's like it's discordant now because people if they absorb this content they expect to see some Yoda floating in the air, you know what I mean?
turns.zh: 现在人们见到你时，因为炒作和现实之间的差距，会不会有问题？现在有些不协调，因为如果人们吸收了这些内容，他们期望看到某种尤达大师漂浮在空中，你明白我的意思吗？

turns.en: Yes, yeah. Like many of you have stopped drinking, but I used to like have the occasional glass of wine. And there was a moment there where I went and met with an information reporter back when I used to meet with reporters, and she said, 'Where are we going to meet?' So I said, 'Oh, let's meet at the wine merchant and we'll get a glass.' She's like, 'What? You drink?'
turns.zh: 是的，没错。就像你们很多人已经戒酒了，但我过去偶尔喜欢喝杯酒。有一次我去见一位信息记者，那时我还经常见记者，她说，‘我们在哪里见面？’我说，‘哦，我们在葡萄酒商店见面，喝一杯。’她说，‘什么？你喝酒？’

transcript.secZh: 炒作与现实之间的差距

transcript.sec: Parenting Philosophy Inspired by David Deutsch

turns.en: You recently did a podcast with Tim Ferriss on parenting. I love this and I bought the book from this guy. Just give a brief overview of this philosophy of parenting.
turns.zh: 你最近和蒂姆·费里斯做了一期关于育儿的播客。我很喜欢，还买了这个人的书。简单介绍一下这个育儿理念吧。

turns.en: Oh, I didn't listen to this. I have to write this down. Tell us this. You're going to love this. This spoke to me, but it was a little crazy. So I'm a big fan of David Deutsch. I think he's basically the smartest living human. He's a scientist who worked on quantum computation and has written a couple of great books. It's about the intersection of the greatest theories we have today: epistemology, the theory of knowledge, evolution, quantum physics, and computation. He wrote 'The Beginning of Infinity' and 'The Fabric of Reality'. I've spent time with him, done podcasts, hired and worked with people around him. His framework has made me smarter. As we age, our brains slow down, but wisdom should substitute for raw horsepower. One thing he pioneered is called 'Taking Children Seriously'. It's the idea that you should take your children seriously, like adults. Give them the same freedom you would give an adult. If you wouldn't speak that way with your spouse or force them to do something, don't force a child. It's only through the latent threat of physical violence that we intimidate children. This resonated with me because I grew up very free. My father wasn't around, my mother didn't have bandwidth to watch us, so I made my own decisions from age five. From age nine, I was telling everyone what to do. I've been homeschooling my own kids, so the philosophy resonated. I found this guy Aaron Stupple on a chat, and he was an incredible expositor of the philosophy. He lives his life with it, 99% as extreme as one can go. His kids can eat all the ice cream and Snickers bars they want, play on the iPad all they want, don't have to go to school if they don't feel like it, dress how they want. Everything is a discussion, negotiation, explanation, just like with a roommate or adult. It's kind of insane and extreme, but I live my own home life in that direction. I'm a very free person: no office, no calendar, I don't send my kids to school, I try not to coerce them.
turns.zh: 哦，我没听这期。我得记下来。跟我们说说吧。你会喜欢的。这很打动我，但有点疯狂。我是大卫·多伊奇的超级粉丝。我觉得他基本上是现世最聪明的人。他是一位研究量子计算的科学家，写了几本好书。内容是关于我们今天最伟大理论的交汇：认识论、知识理论、进化论、量子物理和计算。他写了《无穷的开始》和《真实世界的脉络》。我和他相处过，做过播客，雇佣并与他身边的人共事。他的框架让我更聪明。随着年龄增长，我们的大脑变慢，但智慧应该替代原始智力。他开创的一个理念叫“认真对待孩子”。意思是应该像对待成年人一样认真对待孩子。给他们和成年人同样的自由。如果你不会那样对配偶说话，或强迫配偶做事，也不要强迫孩子。我们只是通过潜在的暴力威胁来恐吓孩子。这让我产生共鸣，因为我成长得非常自由。我父亲不在身边，母亲没空管我们，所以我从五岁就开始自己做决定。九岁起，我就开始指挥别人。我一直在在家教育自己的孩子，所以这个理念很合拍。我找到了一个叫亚伦·斯图普尔的人，他非常擅长阐述这个理念。他几乎百分之百地践行它。他的孩子可以随便吃冰淇淋和士力架，随便玩iPad，不想上学就不上，想穿什么就穿什么。一切都像和室友或成年人一样，通过讨论、协商、解释。这有点疯狂和极端，但我的家庭生活也朝这个方向走。我是个非常自由的人：没有办公室，没有日程表，不送孩子上学，尽量不强迫他们。

turns.en: So your kids, if they wanted to eat a hog dog at 9 PM, you'd be okay? Two nights ago I ordered a hog dog—actually it was a different brand—and we all ate ice cream at 9 PM. They're happy kids. They say 'Dad, I want to be on my iPad, playing Fortnite, leave me alone, I'll go to sleep when I want.' You're like 'okay.' My oldest probably plays iPad nine hours a day. So then your other kid pees in their pants because they're too lazy to walk to the bathroom? They don't do that because they don't like peeing their pants. I understand, but there's a spectrum. Your point of view is 100% allowed with no judgments?
turns.zh: 那么你的孩子，如果晚上9点想吃热狗，你会同意吗？两天前我订了一个热狗——其实是另一个牌子——我们晚上9点都吃了冰淇淋。他们是快乐的孩子。他们说‘爸爸，我想玩iPad，玩堡垒之夜，别管我，我想什么时候睡就什么时候睡。’你就说‘好吧’。我最大的孩子可能一天玩九小时iPad。那你的另一个孩子会因为懒得去厕所而尿裤子吗？他们不会，因为他们不喜欢尿裤子。我明白，但这里有个程度问题。你的观点是百分之百允许，没有任何评判？

turns.en: No, that's not where I am. That's where Aaron is. My rules are a little different. They have to do one hour of math or programming plus two hours of reading every single day. The moment they've done that, they're free creatures. Everything else is a negotiation. We have to persuade them—it's a persuasion, not even a negotiation. And even the hour of math and two hours of reading—really you get 15 to 30 minutes of math, maybe an hour if you're lucky, and half an hour to two hours of reading.
turns.zh: 不，我不是那个意思。那是亚伦的做法。我的规则有点不同。他们每天必须做一小时数学或编程，外加两小时阅读。一旦完成，他们就是自由人。其他一切都是协商。我们必须说服他们——这是说服，甚至不是协商。而且那一小时数学和两小时阅读——实际上数学可能只有15到30分钟，运气好才有一小时，阅读半小时到两小时。

turns.en: And what do you think the long-term consequences of that are? And also, what about health consequences if they make bad decisions, like the ice cream at 9 PM?
turns.zh: 那你觉得这样做的长期后果是什么？还有，如果他们做出不好的决定，比如晚上9点吃冰淇淋，健康方面会有什么后果？

transcript.secZh: 受David Deutsch启发的育儿哲学

turns.en: How do you manage that in your mind? I think whatever age you're at, you're still struggling with your own habits. All of us still eat food and feel guilty or want to eat something we shouldn't. We're always evolving our diets. Kids are the same. My oldest passed on the ice cream last time and said he wants to eat healthier because I finally got through to him. My younger kids will eat it but limited amounts. My middle kid sometimes eats some. If they say something, you enable it but guide them, like, 'Hey, this isn't the choice I would make, but if you want it, I'll try it.' You also have to be careful not to intimidate them or be so overbearing that they view Dad as controlling.
turns.zh: 你心里是怎么处理这件事的？我觉得无论什么年龄、人生什么阶段，你都在和自己的习惯作斗争。我们所有人还是会吃东西感到内疚，或者想吃不该吃的东西。我们一直在调整饮食。孩子也一样。我家老大上次没吃冰淇淋，说想吃得健康点，因为我终于说服了他。小一点的孩子会吃，但限量。中间那个有时会吃一点。如果他们提要求，你会允许但引导他们，比如'嘿，这不是我会做的选择，但如果你想要，我会试试。'你也要小心别吓到他们，别太专横，让他们觉得爸爸控制欲太强。

turns.en: I find this fascinating. What do you think happens to these kids when they're fully formed adults? What is that vision?
turns.zh: 我觉得这很有意思。你觉得这些孩子长大成人后会变成什么样？你的愿景是什么？

turns.en: I try not to. They're going to be who they're going to be. I grew up kind of doing what I wanted. I would rather they have agency than turn out exactly the way I want, because agency is the hardest thing—having control over your own life, making your own decisions. I want them to be happy. I have a very happy household.
turns.zh: 我尽量不去想。他们会成为他们自己。我小时候基本是想做什么就做什么。我宁愿他们有自主权，而不是完全按我的意愿发展，因为自主权是最难的——掌控自己的生活，自己做决定。我希望他们快乐。我的家庭非常幸福。

turns.en: What is Plato's goal? Eudaimonia, right? The happy, fulfilled life. Is that what you want for them?
turns.zh: 柏拉图的目标是什么？幸福（Eudaimonia），对吧？快乐而充实的人生。这是你希望他们拥有的吗？

turns.en: I don't really want anything for them. I just want them to be free and their best selves.
turns.zh: 我其实不期望他们什么。我只希望他们自由，成为最好的自己。

turns.en: God, I want to... Shamet is worrying about details. He's got like 17 kids now. But I love this interview because the guy made a really interesting point: they're going to have to make these decisions at some point, learn the pros and cons, the upside and downside of all these things—eating, iPad. The quicker you get them to have agency to make decisions for themselves with knowledge and to ask questions, the more secure they'll be. I found it fascinating. I like cause and effect, especially in teenagers. Now that I have a teenager, it's good for them to learn: if you don't do your homework, you have a problem, and then you solve it. I like to present it as 'What's your plan?' Anytime they have a problem—8-year-old, 15-year-old—I just say, 'What's your plan to solve this?' Then I hear their plan and let me know if you want to brainstorm. I thought it was a very interesting discussion. Overall, my kids are very happy, the household is happy, everybody gets along, everybody loves each other. Some are way ahead of their peers, nobody is behind in anything that matters, nobody seems unhealthy, no aberrant eating habits, no aberrant behavior. It's all good. Self-correcting.
turns.zh: 天哪，我想……沙马特在担心细节。他好像有17个孩子了。但我喜欢这次采访，因为那人说了一个很有意思的观点：他们迟早要自己做决定，学会权衡利弊——吃东西、玩iPad。你越早让他们带着知识、学会提问、自主做决定，他们就越有安全感。我觉得这很迷人。我喜欢因果关系，尤其是对青少年。现在我有青少年了，让他们明白'如果你不做作业，就会有问题，然后你得解决它'是好事。我喜欢问'你的计划是什么？'无论他们遇到什么问题——8岁还是15岁——我就说'你打算怎么解决？'然后听他们的计划，如果想一起讨论就告诉我。我觉得这是个非常有趣的讨论。总的来说，我的孩子们很快乐，家庭很幸福，大家相处融洽，彼此相爱。有些孩子远超同龄人，没有人在重要方面落后，没有人看起来不健康，没有异常饮食习惯，也没有异常行为。一切都很好，自我纠正。

turns.en: I worry a lot about this iPad situation. I see my kids on an iPad, and unless they're doing an interactive project, if they end up watching... Says the guy who has a video game theme in the background, who probably grew up playing video games nonstop and spends 9 hours a day on his screen. Yeah, it's the same thing. There's a hypocrisy to picking up your phone and saying to your kid, 'No, you can't use your iPad.' I grew up playing video games non-stop until a few years ago. I'm not criticizing the iPad. I was on a computer since I was four, so I get it. But I didn't have the ability to play a 30-minute show, then the next, then sit for two hours watching shows. I was interacting, building stuff, which was different.
turns.zh: 我非常担心iPad这件事。我看到孩子们用iPad，除非他们在做互动项目，否则如果只是看……说这话的人背景是电子游戏主题，他可能从小不停打游戏，每天在屏幕前待9小时。是啊，一回事。自己拿起手机却对孩子说'不许用iPad'，这很虚伪。我从小不停打游戏，直到几年前。我不是批评iPad。我四岁就开始用电脑，所以我理解。但我不能连续看30分钟的节目，接着再看，然后坐两小时一直看。我是在互动、在构建东西，这不一样。

turns.en: We used to control their YouTube access, although now we don't. The only thing I ask is that they put on captions when watching YouTube, so it helps their reading. They learn to read fast. Good tip. One of my kids is really into YouTube; the other two got over it. To the extent they use YouTube, it's mostly to look up videos on their favorite games to get better.
turns.zh: 我们以前会控制他们看YouTube，现在不了。我唯一的要求是看YouTube时打开字幕，这样有助于阅读，他们学阅读更快。好建议。我家一个孩子特别喜欢YouTube，另外两个已经过了那个劲。他们用YouTube主要是查他们喜欢的游戏的视频，想玩得更好。

turns.en: Alright, let's keep moving. We have David Sachs with us. David, give us your philosophy of parenting.
turns.zh: 好了，我们继续。大卫·萨克斯也在。大卫，说说你的育儿哲学。

turns.en: Next item on the docket. Let's talk about some real issues. This show is not a parenting show. I asked David, 'What's your parenting philosophy?' He said, 'Oh, I set up their trust four years ago.' So he's done. Trust is set up, everything's good. Check. You're all set. Let me know how it works out.
turns.zh: 下一个议程。我们来谈谈真正的问题。这个节目不是育儿节目。我问大卫'你的育儿哲学是什么？'他说'哦，我四年前给他们设了信托基金。'所以他搞定了。信托设好了，万事大吉。搞定。你准备好了。告诉我效果如何。

turns.en: Alright, speaking of working out, we've got a vice president who isn't cuckoo for Cocoa Puffs and actually understands AI. JD Vance gave a great speech. I watched it myself. He talked about AI in Paris on Tuesday at the AI Action Summit. He gave a 15-minute banger of a speech. He talked about over-regulating AI and America's intention to dominate this. We happen to have with us Naval, the Zar of AI. Before I go into details, Sax, this speech had a lot of verbage, ideas I've heard before. Maybe tell us how it all came together and how proud you are.
turns.zh: 好了，说到锻炼，我们有一位副总统，他不疯疯癫癫，而且真正理解AI。JD·万斯发表了一篇精彩的演讲。我亲自看了。周二他在巴黎AI行动峰会上谈到了AI。他做了一个15分钟的精彩演讲。他谈到了过度监管AI以及美国主导AI的意图。我们正好请到了AI沙皇纳瓦尔。在我深入细节之前，萨克斯，这篇演讲有很多措辞和想法，我以前听过。也许告诉我们这是怎么形成的，你有多自豪。

turns.en: Having a vice president who understands AI is mind-blowing. He can speak credibly on a topical topic. This was an awesome moment for America.
turns.zh: 有一位懂AI的副总统真是令人震惊。他能就热门话题可信地发言。这对美国来说是一个了不起的时刻。

turns.en: What are you implying, JC? That you workshopped it with him, or that he's smart? Both?
turns.zh: 你在暗示什么，JC？是你和他一起打磨的，还是他聪明？两者都有？

turns.en: The vice president wrote the speech or at least directed all of it. The ideas came from him. I'm not going to take any credit whatsoever.
turns.zh: 副总统写了演讲稿，或者至少指导了全部内容。想法来自他。我不会居功。

turns.en: Okay, well it was on point. Maybe you could talk about it. I agree it was on point. I think it was a very well-crafted and well-delivered speech. He made four main points about the Trump administration's approach to AI. Point one: he's going to ensure that American AI continues to be the gold standard. Fantastic. Check two: he says that the administration...
turns.zh: 好吧，它很到位。也许你可以谈谈。我同意它很到位。我认为这是一篇精心构思、表达出色的演讲。他提出了特朗普政府AI方针的四个要点。第一：他将确保美国AI继续保持黄金标准。太棒了。第二：他说政府……

turns.en: The Vice President gave a speech in Paris with four key points: excessive regulation could kill AI; AI must remain free from ideological bias; the White House will maintain a pro-worker growth path for AI; and the US must win the AI race. What are your thoughts?
turns.zh: 副总统在巴黎发表了演讲，提出了四个关键点：过度监管可能扼杀AI；AI必须保持不受意识形态偏见影响；白宫将维持有利于工人的AI增长路径；美国必须赢得AI竞赛。你有什么看法？

turns.en: I think the Vice President's speech was a breath of fresh air. He started by saying he was there to talk about AI opportunity, not AI safety, which was a shot across the bow. Previous conferences were all about safety—Bletchley Park, EU AI regulation, Biden's executive order. The media also focuses on risks. So his emphasis on opportunity was a much-needed corrective. He then said the US must win the AI race, dominate in chips, models, and applications, unlock energy, and be supportive rather than overregulating. He warned about regulatory capture and said less regulation can create a level playing field. He called out the EU's Digital Services Act as a speed trap for American companies and urged them to be good partners. He also implicitly contrasted with adversarial countries using AI for control. Overall, it was very impressive—polite, optimistic, forward-looking, like what an entrepreneur or smart investor would say.
turns.zh: 我认为副总统的演讲令人耳目一新。他一开始就说要谈论AI机遇而非AI安全，这就像一记当头棒喝。之前的会议都聚焦安全——布莱切利园、欧盟AI法规、拜登的行政令。媒体也关注风险。所以他强调机遇是非常必要的纠正。然后他说美国必须赢得AI竞赛，在芯片、模型和应用上占据主导，释放能源，支持企业而非过度监管。他警告了监管俘获，并说减少监管可以创造公平竞争环境。他点名批评欧盟的《数字服务法》是对美国公司的速度陷阱，并敦促他们成为好伙伴。他还含蓄地对比了用AI进行控制的敌对国。总的来说，非常令人印象深刻——礼貌、乐观、前瞻，就像企业家或聪明投资者会说的话。

transcript.sec: Naval's Views on AI Doomerism and Regulation

turns.en: Did you see the speech? What are your general thoughts on JD Vance representing America and wanting American exceptionalism?
turns.zh: 你看了演讲吗？你对JD·万斯代表美国、追求美国例外主义有什么总体看法？

turns.en: Very surprising, very impressive. I thought he was polite, optimistic, and forward-looking. I agree that America should win and we should not overregulate. I'm not an AI doomer—I don't think AI will end the world. That's a separate conversation. There's a religion that keeps saying the world will end: climate change, AI, asteroids, COVID. It fixates attention. In AI's case, it's overplayed due to incentive bias. Companies that are ahead want to pull up the ladder. They genuinely believe in safety risks, but they're motivated to believe. It's a weird position: they say it's so dangerous that open source shouldn't be allowed, but not so dangerous that a private company can't own it. If it were truly like the Manhattan Project, you wouldn't want one company to own nuclear weapons. Sam Altman famously said AI will capture the light cone of all future value. If that's true, open-source AI and small tech matter a lot. The problem is training models is highly centralized, benefiting from supercomputer clusters. So it's not clear.
turns.zh: 非常令人惊讶，非常令人印象深刻。我认为他礼貌、乐观、前瞻。我同意美国应该赢，我们不应该过度监管。我不是AI末日论者——我不认为AI会毁灭世界。那是另一个话题。有一种宗教不断说世界会终结：气候变化、AI、小行星、新冠。它吸引注意力。在AI的案例中，由于激励偏差，这种说法被过度渲染。领先的公司想抽掉梯子。他们真心相信安全风险，但他们是出于动机去相信。这是一个奇怪的立场：他们说AI太危险了，不应该允许开源，但又没危险到私人公司不能拥有。如果真是曼哈顿计划，你不会想让一家公司拥有核武器。山姆·奥特曼曾著名地说AI将捕获所有未来价值的光锥。如果那是真的，开源AI和小型科技公司就非常重要。问题在于训练模型高度集中，受益于超级计算机集群。所以还不清楚。

transcript.secZh: Naval对AI末日论与监管的看法

turns.en: How can any decentralized model compete? So to me, the real issue boils down to: how do you push AI forward while not having just a very small number of players control the entire thing? We thought we had that solution with the original OpenAI, which was a nonprofit supposed to do for humanity. But now, because they want to incentivize the team and raise money, they have to privatize at least part of it. Although it's not clear to me why they need to privatize the whole thing, like why they need to buy out the nonprofit portion. You could leave a nonprofit portion and have a private portion for incentives. But I think the real challenge is how to keep AI from naturally centralizing, because all the economics and technology underneath are centralizing in nature. If you really think you're going to create God, do you want to put God on a leash with one entity controlling God? That to me is the real fear. I'm not scared of AI; I'm scared of what a very small number of people who control AI do to the rest of us for our own good, because that's how it always works.
turns.zh: 任何去中心化的模型如何竞争？所以对我来说，真正的问题归结为：如何在推动AI前进的同时，不让极少数玩家控制整个领域？我们曾以为最初的OpenAI就是解决方案，它是一个非营利组织，本应为人类服务。但现在，为了激励团队和筹集资金，他们不得不至少将部分私有化。虽然我不清楚为什么他们需要将整个公司私有化，比如为什么要买断非营利部分。你可以保留非营利部分，同时设立私有部分用于激励。但我认为真正的挑战是如何防止AI自然走向中心化，因为底层的所有经济和技术本质上都是中心化的。如果你真的认为你要创造神，你愿意让一个实体牵着神的 leash 吗？对我来说，这才是真正的恐惧。我不害怕AI，我害怕的是控制AI的极少数人为了我们好而对我们做些什么，因为历史总是如此。

turns.en: Well said. Probably should go with the Greek model, having many gods and heroes as well. Freeberg, you heard the JD Vance speech, I assume. What are your thoughts on overregulation and, maybe to Naval's point, one person owning this versus open source?
turns.zh: 说得好。或许应该采用希腊模式，有很多神和英雄。Freeberg，你听了JD Vance的演讲吧？你对过度监管有什么看法？也许结合Naval的观点，一个人拥有这一切与开源相比如何？

turns.en: I think there's this kind of big definition of social balance right now on what I would call techno-optimism and techno-pessimism. Generally, people sort of fall into one of those two camps. Techno-optimists are folks that believe accelerating outcomes with AI, automation, bioengineering, manufacturing, semiconductors, quantum computing, nuclear energy, etc., will usher in an era of abundance by creating leverage, which is what technology gives us. Technology will make things cheaper, deflationary, and give everyone more, so it creates abundance. The challenge is that people who already have a lot worry more about exposure to the downside than they desire the upside. So techno-pessimists are generally like the EU and large parts of the United States, worried about the loss of X, loss of jobs, loss of this and that. Whereas countries like China and India are more excited about the opportunity to create wealth, leverage, and abundance for their people. GDP per capita in the EU is $60,000 a year, in the US $82,000, but in India it's $2,500 and in China $12,600. There's a greater incentive in those countries to manifest upside than for the US and EU, who are more worried about downside. So it's a very difficult social battle underway. I do think over time, those governments and countries and social systems that embrace these technologies are going to become more capitalist and require less government control and intervention in job creation, economy, payments to people, etc. The countries that are more techno-pessimistic will unfortunately find themselves asking for greater government control, intervention in markets, government creating jobs, making payments to people, effectively running the economy. My personal view is that I'm a very strong advocate for technology acceleration because in nearly every case in human history, when a new technology emerged, we largely assumed it worked in the framework of today or yesterday. The automobile came along, and no one envisioned that everyone in the US would own one, requiring new industries like mechanics, car dealerships, roads, etc. It's very hard for us to sit here today and say AI will destroy jobs and predict what it will create. I think we're very likely wrong in whatever estimations we give. The area I think is most underestimated is large technical projects that seem infeasible today but AI can unlock, like habitation in the oceans, creating cities underwater, on the moon, on Mars, or finding new places to live. Those are technically very hard to pull off today, but AI can unlock new industries to enable those transitions. So I think we really get it wrong when we try to assume technology as a transplant for last year or last century, and then we become techno-pessimists because we're worried about losing what we have.
turns.zh: 我认为现在存在一种关于社会平衡的大定义，我称之为技术乐观主义和技术悲观主义。一般来说，人们会落入这两个阵营之一。技术乐观主义者认为，通过AI、自动化、生物工程、制造业、半导体、量子计算、核能等加速成果，将带来一个富足的时代，因为技术创造了杠杆。技术会让东西更便宜、通缩，并给每个人更多，所以它创造了富足。挑战在于，已经拥有很多的人更担心下行风险，而不是渴望上行收益。所以技术悲观主义者通常是欧盟和美国大部分地区，担心失去X、失去工作、失去这个那个。而像中国和印度这样的国家，更热衷于为民众创造财富、杠杆和富足的机会。欧盟的人均GDP是每年6万美元，美国是8.2万，但印度是2500美元，中国是1.26万美元。这些国家有更大的动力去实现上行，而美国和欧盟更担心下行。所以这是一场非常艰难的社会斗争。我认为随着时间的推移，那些拥抱这些技术的政府、国家和社会体系将变得更加资本主义，需要更少的政府控制和干预来创造就业、经济、支付等。而更技术悲观主义的国家将不幸地发现自己要求更多的政府控制、市场干预、政府创造就业、支付给人民，实际上由政府运行经济。我个人的观点是，我强烈支持技术加速，因为在人类历史上几乎每一个案例中，当新技术出现时，我们大多假设它在今天或昨天的框架下运作。汽车出现时，没有人预见到每个美国人都会拥有一辆，从而需要机械师、汽车经销商、道路等新行业。今天我们很难坐在这里说AI会摧毁工作，并预测它会创造什么。我认为我们很可能在给出的任何估计中都是错的。我认为最被低估的领域是那些今天看似不可行但AI可以解锁的大型技术项目，比如海洋居住、在水下建造城市、在月球上建造城市、在火星上建造城市或寻找新的居住地。这些在技术上今天很难实现，但AI可以解锁新的行业来实现这些转变。所以我认为当我们试图将技术视为去年或上个世纪的移植时，我们就大错特错了，然后我们因为担心失去现有的一切而成为技术悲观主义者。

transcript.secZh: 技术乐观主义vs技术悲观主义

turns.en: Are you a techno-pessimist or an optimist? Because you bring up the downside an awful lot here on the program, but you are working every day in a very optimistic way to breed better strawberries and potatoes for folks. So you're a little bit of a...
turns.zh: 你是技术悲观主义者还是乐观主义者？因为你在节目中经常提到下行风险，但你每天都在以非常乐观的方式工作，为人们培育更好的草莓和土豆。所以你有点……

turns.en: No, I have no techno-pessimism whatsoever. I try to point out why the other side is acting the way they are. Got it? Okay, putting it in full context, what I'm trying to highlight is that I think that framework is wrong. I think that frame, the way of operating, creates this fear about worrying about downside, which creates regulation like we see in the EU. As a result, China's GDP will scale while the EU and US will stagnate if that's where they go. That's my assessment or opinion on what will happen.
turns.zh: 不，我完全没有技术悲观主义。我只是试图指出为什么另一方会那样行事。明白吗？好吧，放在完整的背景下，我想强调的是，我认为那个框架是错误的。我认为那个框架、那种运作方式，制造了对下行风险的担忧，从而产生了像我们在欧盟看到的那种监管。结果，如果欧盟和美国走那条路，中国的GDP将增长，而它们将停滞。这就是我对将要发生的事情的评估或看法。

transcript.secZh: 对技术悲观主义的个人立场

turns.en: Chath, you want to wrap this up for us? What are your thoughts on JD? I'll give you two. Okay, the first is I would say this is a really interesting moment where I would call this the tale of two vice presidents. Very early in the Biden administration, Kamala was dispatched on an equally important topic at that time, which was illegal immigration, and she went to Mexico and Guatemala. So you actually have a really interesting A/B test here. You have both vice presidents dealing with what were in that moment incredibly important issues. I think that JD was focused, precise, ambitious. Even the part of the press that was very supportive of Kamala couldn't find a lot of positive things to say about her, and the feedback was that she was meandering, ducking questions, and didn't answer the questions she was asked very well. It's so interesting because it's a bit of a microcosm of what happened over these next four years and her campaign, quite honestly, which you could have taken that window of the...
turns.zh: Chath，你想为我们总结一下吗？你对JD有什么看法？我给你两个。好吧，第一个是，我认为这是一个非常有趣的时刻，我称之为两位副总统的故事。在拜登政府早期，卡玛拉被派去处理当时同样重要的问题，即非法移民，她去了墨西哥和危地马拉。所以这里实际上有一个非常有趣的A/B测试。两位副总统都在处理当时极其重要的问题。我认为JD专注、精准、有抱负。即使是那些非常支持卡玛拉的媒体，也找不到很多正面的话来说她，反馈是她漫无边际、回避问题，没有很好地回答被问到的问题。这很有趣，因为这在某种程度上是接下来四年和她的竞选活动的缩影，老实说，你可以从那个窗口看到……

turns.en: Feedback and unfortunately for her it just continued to be very consistent. So that was one observation I had because I heard him give the speech, I heard her, and I had this kind of moment where I was like, wow, two totally different people.
turns.zh: 反馈对她来说不幸地一直非常一致。这是我观察到的一点，因为我听了他的演讲，也听了她的，那一刻我心想，哇，完全是两个不同的人。

turns.en: The second is on the substance of what JD said. I said this on Tucker and I'll just simplify all of this into a very basic framework: if you want a country to thrive, it needs to have economic supremacy and it needs to have military supremacy. In the absence of those two things, societies crumble. And the only thing that underpins those two things is technological supremacy. And we see this today.
turns.zh: 第二点是关于JD讲话的实质。我在Tucker节目上说过，我会把这一切简化成一个非常基本的框架：如果你希望一个国家繁荣，它需要经济优势和军事优势。没有这两样，社会就会崩溃。而支撑这两者的唯一东西就是技术优势。我们今天就能看到这一点。

turns.en: So on Thursday, what happened with Microsoft? They had a $24 billion contract with the United States Army to deliver some whizbang thing, and they realized that they couldn't deliver it. So what did they do? They went to Anduril. Now why did they go to Anduril? Because Anduril has the technological supremacy to actually execute.
turns.zh: 所以周四，微软发生了什么？他们与美国陆军签订了一份240亿美元的合同，要交付某个尖端玩意儿，但他们意识到自己无法交付。那他们做了什么？他们去找了Anduril。为什么找Anduril？因为Anduril拥有实际执行的技术优势。

turns.en: A few weeks ago, we saw some attempts at technological supremacy from the Chinese with respect to DeepSeek. So I think that this is a very simple existential battle: those who can harness and govern the things that are technologically superior will win, and it will drive economic vibrancy and military supremacy, which then creates safe, strong societies. That's it.
turns.zh: 几周前，我们看到中国在DeepSeek方面尝试技术优势。所以我认为这是一场非常简单的生存之战：那些能够驾驭和管理技术优势事物的人将会获胜，这将推动经济活力和军事优势，从而创造安全、强大的社会。就是这样。

turns.en: So from that perspective, JD nailed it. He saw the forest from the trees. He said exactly what I think needed to be said and put folks on notice that you're either on the ship or you're off the ship. And I think that that was really good.
turns.zh: 所以从这个角度看，JD说得很好。他看到了全局，而不是只见树木不见森林。他说出了我认为需要说的话，并提醒大家，你要么上船，要么下船。我认为这非常好。

transcript.sec: The MAGA-tech divide and JD Vance's tweet

turns.en: Yeah, and there was a little secondary conversation that emerged, Sachs, that I would love to engage you with if you're willing. Which is this civil war, quote unquote, between maybe MAGA 1.0, MAGA 2.0, techies versus the MAGA party like ourselves, and maybe the core MAGA folks. We can pull up the tweet here. In JD's own words, and he's been engaging people, it's very clear that he's writing these tweets. A distinct difference between other politicians and this administration: they just tell you what they think. Here it is: 'I'll try and write something to address this in detail.' This is JD Vance's tweet. But I think this civil war is overstated. Though yes, there are some real divergences between the populace I would describe as MAGA and the techies. But briefly, in general, I dislike substituting American labor for cheap labor. My views on immigration and offshoring flow from this. I like growth and productivity gains, and this informs my view on tech and regulation. When it comes to AI specifically, the risks are number one, overstated (to your point, Naval), or two, difficult to avoid. One of my any real concerns, for instance, is about consumer fraud. That's a valid reason to worry about safety. But the other problem is much worse: if a pure nation is 6 months ahead of the US on AI. Again, I'll try and say more. And this is JD going right at, I think, one of the more controversial topics that the administration is dealing with and has dealt with when it comes to immigration and tech, because these two things are dovetailing each other. If we lose millions of driver jobs, which we will in the next 10 years, just like we lost millions of cashier jobs, well, that's going to impact how our nation and many of the voters look at the border and immigration. We might not be able to let as many people immigrate here if we're losing millions of jobs to AI and self-driving cars. What are your thoughts on him engaging this directly, Sachs?
turns.zh: 是的，还有一个小插曲，Sachs，如果你愿意的话，我很想和你聊聊。那就是所谓的MAGA 1.0、MAGA 2.0、科技派与像我们这样的MAGA派，以及核心MAGA派之间的内战。我们可以把推文调出来。用JD自己的话说，他一直在与人互动，很明显这些推文是他自己写的。与其他政客和本届政府的一个显著区别是：他们直接告诉你他们的想法。推文是：‘我会试着写点东西来详细讨论这个问题。’这是JD万斯的推文。但我认为这场内战被夸大了。虽然确实，我称之为MAGA的民众与科技派之间存在一些真正的分歧。但简而言之，总的来说，我不喜欢用廉价劳动力取代美国劳动力。我对移民和外包的看法源于此。我喜欢增长和生产力提升，这影响了我对科技和监管的看法。具体到AI，风险要么是被夸大了（正如你所说，Naval），要么是难以避免。我真正担心的一个例子是消费者欺诈。这是担心安全的合理理由。但另一个问题更严重：如果某个纯粹的国家在AI上领先美国6个月。我会再试着多说一些。而JD直接触及了，我认为，本届政府正在处理且已经处理过的最具争议的话题之一，即移民和科技，因为这两件事是相互关联的。如果我们失去数百万司机的工作，这在未来10年内会发生，就像我们失去数百万收银员的工作一样，那将影响我们国家和许多选民对边境和移民的看法。如果我们因为AI和自动驾驶汽车而失去数百万个工作岗位，我们可能无法让那么多人移民到这里。你对他直接讨论这个问题有什么看法，Sachs？

turns.en: Well, the first point he's making there is about wage pressure, right? Which is when you throw open our borders or you throw open American markets to products that can be made in foreign countries by much cheaper labor that's not held to the same standards, the same minimum wage, or the same union rules, or the same safety standards that American labor is, and has a huge cost advantage, then you're creating wage pressure for American workers. And he's opposed to that. And I think that is an important point because I think the way that the media or neoliberals like to portray this argument is that somehow MAGA's resistance to unlimited immigration is somehow based on xenophobia or something like that. No, it's based on bread and butter, kitchen table issues, which is if you have this ridiculous open border policy, it's inevitably going to create a lot of wage pressure for people at the bottom of the pyramid. So I think JD is making that argument.
turns.zh: 他提出的第一点是关于工资压力，对吧？当你开放边境，或者开放美国市场给那些由廉价劳动力在外国生产的产品，这些劳动力不受同样的标准、同样的最低工资、同样的工会规则或同样的安全标准约束，并且拥有巨大的成本优势时，你就给美国工人带来了工资压力。他反对这一点。我认为这一点很重要，因为媒体或新自由主义者喜欢把这种论点描绘成MAGA对无限移民的抵制是基于仇外心理之类的东西。不，这是基于实实在在的、餐桌上的问题：如果你有这种荒谬的开放边境政策，它必然会给金字塔底层的人带来巨大的工资压力。所以我认为JD是在提出这个论点。

turns.en: But, and this is point two, he's saying I'm not against productivity growth. So technology is good because it enables all of our workers to improve their productivity, and that should result in better wages because workers can produce more, the value of their labor goes up if they have more tools to be productive. So there's no contradiction there. And I think he's explaining why there isn't a contradiction.
turns.zh: 但是，这是第二点，他说我不反对生产力增长。所以技术是好的，因为它使我们所有的工人能够提高生产力，这应该会带来更高的工资，因为工人可以生产更多，如果他们拥有更多提高生产力的工具，他们的劳动价值就会上升。所以这里没有矛盾。我认为他在解释为什么没有矛盾。

turns.en: A point I would add, he doesn't make this point in that tweet, but I would add is that one of the problems that we've had over the last, I don't know, 30 years is that we have had tremendous productivity growth in the US, but labor has not been able to capture it. All that benefit has basically gone to capital or to companies. And I think a big part of the reason why is because we've had this largely unrestricted immigration policy. So I think if you were to tamp down on immigration, if you were to stop the illegal immigration, then labor might be able to capture more of the benefits of productivity growth, and that would be a good thing. It'd be a more equitable distribution of the gains from productivity and from technology, and that I think would help tamp down this growing conflict that you see between technologists and the rest of the country, or certainly the heartland of the country.
turns.zh: 我想补充一点，他在那条推文中没有提到这一点，但我想补充的是，过去30年来我们面临的一个问题是，美国的生产力增长巨大，但劳动力却无法从中获益。所有这些好处基本上都流向了资本或公司。我认为一个很大的原因是我们实行了基本上不受限制的移民政策。所以我认为，如果你限制移民，如果你停止非法移民，那么劳动力或许能够获得更多生产力增长的好处，这将是一件好事。这将更公平地分配生产力和技术带来的收益，我认为这将有助于平息你看到的科技界与美国其他地区（至少是腹地）之间日益增长的冲突。

turns.en: Naval, this is a... okay, you want to add anything else? David, sorry.
turns.zh: Naval，这是一个……好吧，你想补充什么吗？David，抱歉。

turns.en: Well, I think just the final point he makes in that tweet is that he talks about how we live in a world in which there are other countries that are competitive, and specifically he doesn't mention China, but he says we have a pure competitor, and it's going to be a much worse world if they end up being six months ahead of us on AI rather than six months behind. That is a really important point to keep in mind. I think that the whole Paris AI Summit took place against the backdrop of this recognition, because just a few weeks ago we had DeepSeek, and it's really clear that China is not a year behind us; they're hot on our heels, or only maybe months behind us. So if we hobble ourselves with unnecessary regulations, if we make it more difficult for our AI companies to compete, that doesn't mean that China's going to follow suit and copy us; they're going to take advantage of that fact and they're going to win.
turns.zh: 嗯，我认为他在那条推文中提出的最后一点是，他谈到我们生活在一个有其他竞争国家的世界里，具体来说他没有提到中国，但他说我们有一个纯粹的竞争对手，如果他们最终在AI上领先我们六个月而不是落后六个月，世界将会糟糕得多。这是一个非常重要的观点，需要牢记。我认为整个巴黎AI峰会都是在认识到这一点的背景下举行的，因为就在几周前，我们看到了DeepSeek，很明显中国并不比我们落后一年；他们紧追不舍，可能只落后我们几个月。所以如果我们用不必要的监管束缚自己，如果我们让我们的AI公司更难竞争，那并不意味着中国会效仿我们；他们会利用这一点并获胜。

turns.en: All right, Naval, this seems to be one of the main issues of our time. Four of the five people on this podcast right now are immigrants. So we have this amazing... in America, this is a country built by immigrants for immigrants. Do you think that should change now in the face of job displacement?
turns.zh: 好了，Naval，这似乎是我们这个时代的主要问题之一。现在这个播客的五个人中有四个是移民。所以我们有这个惊人的……在美国，这是一个由移民为移民建立的国家。你认为面对工作岗位流失，现在应该改变这一点吗？

transcript.secZh: MAGA-科技分歧与JD Vance的推文

turns.en: Destruction which I know you've been tracking self-driving pretty acutely. We both have an interest there. I think over the years, you know, what's the solution here? If we're going to see a bunch of job displacement, which will happen for certain jobs, we all kind of know that. Should we shut the border and not let the next Naval, Chamath, Max, and Friedberg into the country?
turns.zh: 毁灭，我知道你一直在密切关注自动驾驶。我们俩都对这感兴趣。这些年来，你知道，解决方案是什么？如果我们将会看到大量工作岗位被取代——某些工作肯定会这样，我们都心知肚明——我们是否应该关闭边境，不让下一个 Naval、Chamath、Max 和 Friedberg 进入这个国家？

turns.en: Well, let me declare my biases up front. I'm a first-generation immigrant. I moved here when I was 9 years old, rather my parents did, and then I naturalized as a citizen. So obviously I'm in favor of some level of immigration. That said, I'm assimilated. I consider myself an American first and foremost. I bleed red, white, and blue. I believe in the Bill of Rights and the Constitution, first, second, fourth, and all the proper amendments. I get up there every July 4th and I deliberately defend the Second Amendment on Twitter, at which point half my followers go bananas, you know, because I'm not supposed to. I'm supposed to be a good immigrant, right, and carry the usual set of coherent leftist policies, globalist policies. So I think that legal, high-skill immigration with room and time for assimilation makes sense. You want to have a brain drain of the best and brightest coming to the freest country in the world to build technology and to help civilization move forward. And as Chth was saying, economic power and military power are downstream of technology. In fact, even culture is downstream of technology. Look at what the birth control pill did to culture, or what the automobile did to culture, or what radio and television did to culture, and then the internet. So technology drives everything. And if you look at wealth, wealth is a set of physical transformations that you can affect, and that's a combination of capital and knowledge. The bigger input to that is knowledge. And so the US has become the home of knowledge creation thanks to bringing in the best and brightest. You could even argue DeepSeek: part of the reason why we lost that is because a bunch of those kids studied in the US but then we sent them back home, right?
turns.zh: 嗯，我先声明一下我的立场。我是第一代移民，9岁时来到这里，确切说是我的父母带我来的，后来我归化成了公民。所以显然我支持一定程度的移民。但话说回来，我已经同化了。我首先认为自己是个美国人，我流着红白蓝的血。我信仰《权利法案》和宪法，第一、第二、第四修正案等等。每年7月4日我都会站出来，故意在推特上捍卫第二修正案，这时我一半的粉丝就会发疯，你知道，因为我不该这么做。我应该是个好移民，对吧，带着一套连贯的左派政策、全球主义政策。所以我认为，合法的高技能移民，并给予同化的空间和时间，是合理的。你希望把最优秀最聪明的人才吸引到世界上最自由的国家，来建设技术，推动文明前进。正如Chth所说，经济实力和军事实力都源于技术。事实上，连文化也源于技术。看看避孕药对文化做了什么，汽车对文化做了什么，广播和电视对文化做了什么，还有互联网。所以技术驱动一切。如果你看财富，财富是一系列你能影响的物理转变，是资本和知识的结合。其中更大的投入是知识。所以美国成为了知识创造的家园，得益于引进了最优秀最聪明的人才。你甚至可以争论说DeepSeek：我们失去它的部分原因就是那些孩子在美国学习，但我们却把他们送回了家，对吧？

turns.en: Is that actually accurate? That they were... yeah, yeah, yeah, some a few of them really. Oh my God, that's like exhibit A. Wow, I didn't know that.
turns.zh: 这真的准确吗？他们……是的，是的，是的，其中一些确实如此。哦天哪，这就像证据A。哇，我不知道。

turns.en: So I think you absolutely have to split skilled, assimilated immigration, which is a small set, and it has to be both: they have to be skilled and they have to become Americans. That oath is not meaningless, right? It has to mean something. So skilled, assimilated immigration, you have to separate that from just open borders, whoever can wander in, just come on in. That latter part makes no sense. If the Biden administration had only been letting in people with 150 IQs, we wouldn't have this debate right now. The reason why we're having this debate is because they just opened the border and let millions and millions of people in. It was to their advantage to conflate legal and illegal immigration. So every time you'd be like, 'Well, we can't just open the border,' say 'What about Elon? What about this?' and they would just parade. If they were just letting Elon and Jensen and Friedberg in, we wouldn't be having the same conversation today. The correlation between open borders and wage suppression is irrefutable. We know that data. And I think that the Democrats, for whatever logic, committed an incredible error in basically undermining their core cohort.
turns.zh: 所以我认为你绝对必须把熟练的、同化的移民分开，这是一个小群体，而且必须两者兼备：他们必须熟练，而且必须成为美国人。那个誓言不是无意义的，对吧？它必须有意义。所以熟练的、同化的移民，你必须把它和开放边境分开——谁都可以溜进来，随便进。后一部分毫无意义。如果拜登政府只允许智商150的人入境，我们现在就不会有这场辩论。我们之所以有这场辩论，是因为他们只是开放了边境，让数百万人涌入。把合法和非法移民混为一谈对他们有利。所以每次你都会说，‘嗯，我们不能只是开放边境’，然后说‘那Elon呢？这个呢？’他们就会列出来。如果他们只让Elon、Jensen和Friedberg进来，我们今天就不会有同样的对话。开放边境和工资压制之间的相关性是不可辩驳的。我们知道那些数据。而且我认为民主党，无论出于什么逻辑，犯了一个巨大的错误，基本上削弱了他们的核心群体。

turns.en: I want to go back to what you said because I think it's super important. There is a new political calculus on the field, and I agree with you. I think that the three cohorts of the future are: the asset-light working and middle class, that's cohort number one, there are probably 100 to 150 million of those folks; then there are patriotic business owners; and then there are leaders in innovation. Those are the three. And I think that what MAGA gets right is they found the middle ground that intersects those three cohorts of people. And so every time you see this sort of left versus right dichotomy, it's totally miscast and it sounds discordant to so many of us because that's not how any of us identify, right? And I think that that's a very important observation because the policies that we adopt will need to reflect those three cohorts. What is the common ground amongst those three? And on that point, Naval is right: there's not a lot that those three would say is wrong with a very targeted form of extremely useful legal immigration of very, very, very smart people who agree to assimilate and be a part of America.
turns.zh: 我想回到你刚才说的，因为我认为这非常重要。现在有一种新的政治算法，我同意你的看法。我认为未来的三个群体是：轻资产的工薪和中产阶级，这是第一群体，大概有1到1.5亿人；然后是爱国的企业主；再然后是创新领袖。就是这三个。我认为MAGA做对的地方是，他们找到了这三个群体的交集。所以每次你看到这种左对右的二分法，它完全被误判了，对我们很多人来说听起来不和谐，因为我们没有人这样认同自己，对吧？我认为这是一个非常重要的观察，因为我们采取的政策需要反映这三个群体。这三者之间的共同点是什么？在这一点上，Naval是对的：这三个群体不会对一种非常有针对性的、极其有用的合法移民形式——让非常非常聪明的人同意同化并成为美国的一部分——说太多不对的地方。

turns.en: I mean, I'm so glad you said it the way you said it. Like, I remember growing up where my parents would try to pretend that they were in Sri Lanka, and sometimes I would get so frustrated. I'm like, 'If you want to be in Sri Lanka, go back to Sri Lanka. I want to be Canadian,' because it was easier for me to make friends, it was easier for me to have a life. I was trying my best. I wanted to be Canadian. And then when I moved to the United States 25 years ago, I wanted to be American. And I feel that I'm American now, and I'm proud to be an American. And I think that's what you want: you want people that embrace it. It doesn't mean that we can't dress up in a show of saris every now and then, but the point is, like, what do you believe and where is your loyalty?
turns.zh: 我的意思是，我很高兴你这么说。就像，我记得小时候我父母会假装他们在斯里兰卡，有时我会非常沮丧。我说，‘如果你想在斯里兰卡，就回斯里兰卡去。我想成为加拿大人，’因为对我来说交朋友更容易，生活更容易。我尽了最大努力。我想成为加拿大人。然后25年前我搬到美国时，我想成为美国人。我现在感觉自己是美国人，我为自己是美国人而自豪。我认为这就是你想要的：你想要拥抱它的人。这不意味着我们不能偶尔穿上纱丽表演，但关键是，你相信什么，你的忠诚在哪里？

turns.en: Friedberg, we used to have this concept of a melting pot, of assimilation, and that was a good thing. Then it became cultural appropriation. We kind of made a right turn here. Where do you stand on this: recruiting the best and brightest and forcing them to assimilate, making sure that they're down? Jason, find the people that care to be here.
turns.zh: Friedberg，我们曾经有一个大熔炉的概念，即同化，那是一件好事。然后它变成了文化挪用。我们在这里有点右转了。你对此持什么立场：招募最优秀最聪明的人，强迫他们同化，确保他们认同？Jason，找到那些在乎这里的人。

turns.en: Yeah, let me... I reject the premise. This whole conversation... wait, wait, hold on. Look, I'm a first-generation American who moved here when I was five and became a citizen when I was 10. And yes, I'm fully American, and that's the only country I have any loyalty to. But the premise that I reject here is that somehow an AI conversation leads to an immigration conversation because millions of jobs are going to be lost. We don't know that that's also true. I agree you're making a huge assumption buying into the doomsday of jobs that is not... those are fact not evidence. I think any jobs have been lost by AI? Let's be real. We've had AI for two and a half years, and I think it's great, but so far it's a better search engine and it helps high school kids cheat on their essays. I mean, you don't believe that self-driving is coming? Hold on a second, Sacks. You don't believe that millions... but hold on, those driver jobs weren't even there 10 years ago. Uber came along and created all these driver jobs, DoorDash created all these driver jobs. So what technology does? Yes, technology destroys jobs, but it replaces them with opportunities that are even better. And then either you can go capture that opportunity yourself, or an entrepreneur will come along and create something that allows you to capture those.
turns.zh: 是的，让我……我拒绝这个前提。整个对话……等等，等等，等一下。听着，我是第一代美国人，5岁时搬到这里，10岁时成为公民。是的，我完全是美国人，那是我唯一忠诚的国家。但我拒绝的前提是，不知何故，AI对话导致了移民对话，因为数百万工作岗位将会消失。我们不知道那是否也是真的。我同意你做了一个巨大的假设，相信工作的末日，那不是……那些是事实，不是证据。我认为有任何工作被AI取代了吗？现实点吧。我们已经有AI两年半了，我认为它很棒，但到目前为止它只是一个更好的搜索引擎，帮助高中生作弊写论文。我的意思是，你不相信自动驾驶正在到来？等一下，Sacks。你不相信数百万……但等等，那些司机工作在10年前甚至不存在。Uber出现了，创造了所有这些司机工作，DoorDash创造了所有这些司机工作。所以技术做了什么？是的，技术摧毁了工作，但它用更好的机会取代了它们。然后要么你自己去抓住那个机会，要么一个企业家会出现，创造一些东西让你抓住那些机会。

transcript.sec: AI as a productivity tool and job creation

turns.en: AI is a productivity tool. It increases the productivity of a worker, allowing them to do more creative work and less repetitive work. As such, it makes them more valuable. Yes, there is some retraining involved, but not a lot. These are natural language computers; you can talk to them in plain English and they talk back to you in plain English. But I think David is absolutely right. I think we will see job creation by AI that will be as fast or faster than job destruction. You saw this even with the internet. Like YouTube came along, look at all these YouTube streamers and influencers that didn't used to be a job. New jobs, really opportunities, because 'job' is a wrong word. 'Job' implies someone else has to give it to me, and it's sort of handed out, a zero-sum game. Forget all that. It's opportunities. After COVID, look at how many people are making money by working from home in mysterious little ways on the internet that you can't even quite grasp.
turns.zh: AI 是一种生产力工具。它提高了工人的生产力，让他们能做更多创造性工作，减少重复性劳动。因此，它让他们变得更有价值。是的，这需要一些再培训，但不多。这些是自然语言计算机；你可以用简单的英语和它们交谈，它们也用简单的英语回应你。但我认为 David 完全正确。我认为我们会看到 AI 创造就业的速度与破坏就业的速度一样快，甚至更快。你在互联网上就看到了这一点。比如 YouTube 出现后，看看所有这些 YouTube 主播和网红，这以前根本不是一份工作。新工作，实际上是新机遇，因为“工作”这个词用错了。“工作”意味着别人必须给我一份工作，像是施舍，是零和游戏。忘掉这些吧。这是机遇。疫情之后，看看有多少人通过在家工作、以各种你甚至无法理解的神秘方式在互联网上赚钱。

turns.en: Here's the way I categorize it. Whenever you have a new technology, you get productivity gains. You get some job disruption, meaning that part of your job may go away, but then you get other parts that are new and hopefully more elevated, more interesting. And then there is some job loss. I just think that the third category will follow the historical trend, which is that the first two categories are always bigger, and you end up with more net productivity and more net wealth creation. And we've seen no evidence to date that that's not going to be the case. Now it's true that AI is about to get more powerful. You're going to see a whole new wave of what are called agents this year. Agentic products are able to do more for you. But there's no evidence yet that those things are going to be completely unsupervised and replace people's jobs. So I think that we have to see how this technology evolves. And I think one of the mistakes of, let's call it the European approach, is assuming that you can predict the future with perfect accuracy, with such good accuracy that you can create regulations today that are going to avoid all these risks in the future. And we just don't know enough yet to be able to do that. That's level of certainty.
turns.zh: 我是这样分类的。每当出现新技术，你都会获得生产力提升。你会遇到一些工作颠覆，意味着你工作的某部分可能会消失，但随后你会得到新的部分，希望是更高级、更有趣的。然后还有一些工作流失。我只是认为第三类会遵循历史趋势，即前两类总是更大，最终你会获得更多的净生产力和净财富创造。迄今为止，我们没有看到任何证据表明情况不会如此。诚然，AI 即将变得更强大。今年你会看到一波被称为智能体的新产品。智能体式产品能为你做更多事情。但目前还没有证据表明这些东西会完全无人监督并取代人们的工作。所以我认为我们必须观察这项技术如何发展。我认为所谓的欧洲方法的一个错误是，假设你可以完美准确地预测未来，准确到你可以今天制定法规来避免未来所有风险。而我们目前还了解得不够，无法做到这一点。那是确定性的问题。

turns.en: I agree with you. And the companies that are promulgating that view, as Naval said, those that have an economic vested interest in at least convincing the next incremental investor that this could be true, because they want to make the claim that all the money should go to them so they can Hoover up all the economic gains. And that's the part of the cycle we're in. So if you actually stratify these reactions, there's the small startup companies in AI that believe there's a productivity leap to be had and that there's going to be prosperity. Everybody on the sidelines watching. And then a few companies that have an extremely vested interest in them being a gatekeeper because they need to raise the next 30 or 40 billion dollars, trying to convince people that that's true. And if you view it through that lens, you're right. We have not accomplished anything yet that proves that this is going to be cataclysmically bad. And if anything, right now history would tell you it's probably going to be like the past, which is generally productive and enriches society.
turns.zh: 我同意你的看法。那些宣扬这种观点的公司，正如 Naval 所说，那些有经济利益的公司，至少是为了说服下一个增量投资者这可能成真，因为他们想声称所有资金都应该流向他们，这样他们就能攫取所有经济收益。这就是我们目前所处的周期阶段。所以如果你真的对这些反应进行分层，有小型 AI 初创公司相信生产力飞跃即将到来，繁荣即将来临。所有人都在旁观。然后有几家公司有极大的既得利益成为守门人，因为他们需要筹集下一个 300 或 400 亿美元，试图让人们相信这是真的。如果你从这个角度看，你是对的。我们还没有取得任何成就来证明这将是灾难性的。相反，历史会告诉你，它很可能像过去一样，总体上是生产性的，并丰富社会。

turns.en: Yeah, and just to bring it back to JD's speech, which is where we started, I think it was a quintessentially American speech in the sense that he said we should be optimistic about the opportunities here, which I think is basically right. And we want to lead, we want to take advantage of this, we don't want to hobble it. We don't even fully know what it's going to be yet. We are going to center workers, we want to be pro-worker. And I think that if there are downsides for workers, then we can mitigate those things in the future. But it's too early to say that we know what the program should be. It's more about a statement of values at this point.
turns.zh: 是的，回到 JD 的演讲，那是我们的起点。我认为那是一个典型的美国式演讲，他说我们应该对这里的机遇持乐观态度，我认为这基本上是正确的。我们想要引领，想要利用这一点，不想束缚它。我们甚至还不完全知道它会是什么。我们将以工人为中心，我们希望支持工人。我认为如果对工人有不利影响，我们可以在未来缓解这些问题。但现在说我们知道该怎么做还为时过早。这更多是关于价值观的声明。

turns.en: Do you think it's too early, Freeberg? Given optimists and all these robots being created, what we're seeing in self-driving, you've talked about the ramp-up with Waymo, to actually say we will not see millions of jobs and millions of people get displaced from those jobs. What do you think, Freeberg? I'm curious your thoughts because that is the counterargument.
turns.zh: Freeberg，你觉得现在说还为时过早吗？考虑到乐观主义者以及所有这些正在被创造的机器人，我们在自动驾驶中看到的，你谈到过 Waymo 的加速发展，实际上要说我们不会看到数百万个工作岗位和数百万人被这些工作取代。你怎么看，Freeberg？我很好奇你的想法，因为那是反对的观点。

turns.en: My experience in the workplace is that AI tools that are doing things that an analyst or knowledge worker was doing with many hours in the past is allowing them to do something in minutes. That doesn't mean that they spend the rest of the day doing nothing. What's great for our business and for other businesses like ours that can leverage AI tools is that those individuals can now do more. And so our throughput, our productivity as an organization has gone up, and we can now create more things faster. So whatever the product is that my company makes, we can now make more things more quickly, we can do more development. You're seeing it on the ground, correct? At a hollow? And I'm seeing it on the ground. And I don't think that this transplantation of how bad AI will be for jobs is the right framing, as much as it is about an acceleration of productivity. And this is why I go back to the point about GDP per capita and GDP growth. Countries, societies, areas that are interested, or industries that are interested in accelerating output, in accelerating productivity, the ability to make stuff and sell stuff, are going to rapidly embrace these tools because it allows them to do more with less. And I think that's what I really see on the ground. And then the second point I'll make is the one that I mentioned earlier, and I'll wrap up with a third point. Which is I think we're underestimating the new industries that will emerge drastically, dramatically. There is going to be so much new stuff that we are not really thinking deeply about right now that we could do a whole other two-hour brainstorming session on what AI unlocks in terms of large-scale projects that are traditionally or typically today held back because of the constraints on the technical feasibility of these projects. And that ranges from accelerating new semiconductor technology to quantum computing, to energy systems, to transportation, to habitation, etc., etc. There's all sorts of transformations in every industry that are possible as these tools come online, and that will spurn insane new industries. The most important point is the third one, which is we don't know the overlap of job loss and job creation if there is one. And so the rate at which these new technologies impact and create new markets, but I think Naval is right. I think that what happens in capitalism and in free societies is that capital and people rush to fill the hole of new opportunities that emerge because of AI, and that those grow more quickly than the old bubbles deflate. So if there's a deflationary effect in terms of job need in other industries, I think the loss will happen slower than the rush to take advantage of creating new things will happen.
turns.zh: 我在工作场所的经验是，AI 工具正在做分析师或知识工作者过去需要很多小时才能完成的事情，现在只需几分钟。这并不意味着他们一天剩下的时间无所事事。对我们的业务以及像我们这样可以利用 AI 工具的其他企业来说，好处是这些人现在可以做更多事情。因此，我们组织的吞吐量、生产力提高了，我们现在可以更快地创造更多东西。所以无论我的公司生产什么产品，我们现在可以更快地制造更多东西，我们可以做更多的开发。你在实地看到了，对吧？在 Hollow？我也在实地看到了。我不认为这种关于 AI 对就业有多糟糕的移植是恰当的框架，它更多是关于生产力的加速。这就是为什么我回到人均 GDP 和 GDP 增长的观点。那些有兴趣加速产出、加速生产力、制造和销售能力的国家、社会、地区或行业，将迅速拥抱这些工具，因为它们允许用更少的资源做更多的事情。我认为这就是我在实地真正看到的。然后我要说的第二点是我之前提到的，我将以第三点结束。我认为我们大大低估了将会急剧、戏剧性出现的新行业。将会有很多我们现在没有深入思考的新事物，我们可以再花两个小时进行头脑风暴，讨论 AI 在大型项目方面解锁了什么，这些项目传统上或通常因为技术可行性限制而被搁置。这包括从加速新半导体技术到量子计算、能源系统、交通、居住等等。随着这些工具上线，每个行业都有可能发生各种转型，这将催生疯狂的新行业。最重要的一点是第三点，即我们不知道失业和就业创造之间的重叠（如果有的话）。因此，这些新技术影响和创造新市场的速度，但我认为 Naval 是对的。我认为在资本主义和自由社会中，资本和人们会涌向 AI 带来的新机遇，这些机遇的增长速度比旧泡沫的破灭更快。所以如果其他行业的就业需求出现通缩效应，我认为损失的速度会慢于利用创造新事物的热潮。

transcript.secZh: AI作为生产力工具与就业创造

transcript.sec: AI and Job Displacement vs. Opportunity

turns.en: The other side, so my bet is probably on the order of I think new things will be created faster than old things will be lost. I think actually as a quick side note to that, the fastest way to help somebody get a job right now, if you know somebody in the market who's looking for a job, the best thing you can do is say, 'Hey, go download the AI tools and just start talking to them, just start using them in any way.' And then you can walk into any employer in almost any field and say, 'Hey, I understand AI,' and they'll hire you on the spot.
turns.zh: 另一方面，我的赌注大概是这样的：我认为新事物的创造速度会快于旧事物的消失速度。顺便提一句，现在帮人找工作最快的方法，如果你认识正在求职的人，最好的建议就是：‘嘿，去下载AI工具，开始跟它们对话，用各种方式使用它们。’然后你几乎可以走进任何领域的任何雇主那里说：‘嘿，我懂AI’，他们就会当场录用你。

turns.en: Exactly. Naval, you and I watched this happen. We had a front row seat to it back in the day when you were doing Venture Hacks and I was doing Open Angel Forum. We had to like fight to find five or 10 companies a month. Then the cost of running these companies went down, they went down massively from five million to start a company to two, then to 250k, then to 100K. I think what we're seeing is like three things concurrently: you're going to see all these jobs go away for automation, self-driving cars, cashier, etc. But we're also going to see static team size at places like Google; they're just not hiring because they're just having the existing bloated employee base learn the tools. But I don't know if you're seeing this, the number of startups able to get a product to market with two or three people and get to a million in revenue is booming. What are you seeing in the startup landscape?
turns.zh: 没错。Naval，你我都亲眼目睹了这一切。当年你做Venture Hacks、我做Open Angel Forum的时候，我们每个月要费很大劲才能找到五到十家公司。然后创办公司的成本大幅下降，从五百万降到两百万，再到二十五万，再到十万。我认为我们同时看到了三件事：你会看到所有这些工作因自动化、自动驾驶汽车、收银员等而消失。但我们也看到像谷歌这样的公司团队规模停滞不前；他们不招人，只是让现有臃肿的员工学习工具。但不知道你是否注意到，能够用两三个人将产品推向市场并达到百万美元收入的初创公司数量正在激增。你在创业领域看到了什么？

turns.en: Definitely what you're saying in that there's leverage, but at the same time, I think the more interesting part is that new startups are enabled that could not exist otherwise. My last startup, AirChat, could not have existed without AI because we needed transcription and translation. Even the current thing I'm working on, it's not an AI company, but it cannot exist without AI; it is relying on AI. Even at AngelList, we're significantly adopting AI. Like everywhere you turn, it's more opportunity, more opportunity, more opportunity. And people like to go on Twitter or the artist formerly known as Twitter and basically they like to exaggerate, like 'Oh my God, we've hit AGI,' 'Oh my God, I just replaced all my mid-level engineers,' 'Oh my God, I've stopped hiring.' To me, that's like moronic. The two valid ones are the one entrepreneur shows where there's like one guy or one gal and they're like scaling up like crazy thanks to AI, or there are people who are embracing AI and being like 'I need to hire and I need to hire anyone who can even spell AI, like anyone who's even used AI, just come on in, come on in.' Again, I would say the easiest way to see that AI is not taking jobs but creating opportunities is: go brush up on your skills, learn a little bit, watch a few videos, use the AI, tinker with it, and then go reapply for that job that rejected you and watch how they pull you in.
turns.zh: 确实如你所说，存在杠杆效应，但与此同时，我认为更有趣的是，新的初创公司得以成立，否则它们根本不可能存在。我上一家创业公司AirChat如果没有AI就无法存在，因为我们需要转录和翻译。即使我现在正在做的事情，虽然不是AI公司，但没有AI也无法存在，它依赖AI。甚至在AngelList，我们也在大量采用AI。无论你转向哪里，都是更多的机会、更多的机会、更多的机会。而人们喜欢在Twitter或那个前身为Twitter的平台上夸大其词，比如‘天哪，我们已经实现了AGI’，‘天哪，我刚替换了我所有的中级工程师’，‘天哪，我已经停止招聘了’。对我来说，这很愚蠢。只有两种合理的情况：一种是创业者展示一个人或一个女孩，他们借助AI疯狂扩张；另一种是那些拥抱AI的人说‘我需要招聘，我需要招聘任何会拼写AI的人，任何使用过AI的人，都请进来，请进来。’再次强调，要看到AI不是在夺走工作而是在创造机会，最简单的方法是：去提升你的技能，学一点东西，看一些视频，使用AI，摆弄它，然后重新申请那份拒绝过你的工作，看看他们如何把你拉进来。

turns.en: In 2023, an economist named Richard Baldwin said: 'AI won't take your job, it's someone using AI that will take your job because they know how to use it better than you.' And that's kind of become a meme, and you see it floating around X. But I think there's a lot of truth in that. You know, as long as you remain adaptive and you keep learning and you learn how to take advantage of these tools, you should do better. And if you wall yourself off from the technology and don't take advantage of it, that's when you put yourself at risk. Another way to think about it is these are natural language computers, so everyone who's intimidated by computers before should no longer be intimidated. You don't need to program anymore in some esoteric language or learn some obscure mathematics to be able to use these; you can just talk to them and they talk back to you. That's magic. The new programming language is English.
turns.zh: 2023年，经济学家理查德·鲍德温说：‘AI不会夺走你的工作，而是会使用AI的人夺走你的工作，因为他们比你更懂得如何使用它。’这已经成了一个梗，你在X上经常能看到。但我认为这很有道理。你知道，只要你保持适应能力，不断学习，学会利用这些工具，你就会做得更好。如果你把自己与科技隔绝，不去利用它，那才是你把自己置于危险之中。另一种思考方式是，这些是自然语言计算机，所以以前对电脑感到畏惧的人不再需要畏惧了。你不再需要用某种晦涩的语言编程或学习某种深奥的数学才能使用它们；你只需跟它们说话，它们就会回应你。这就是魔法。新的编程语言是英语。

turns.en: Chat, you want to wrap us up here on this opportunity slash displacement slash chaos? I was gonna say this before, but I'm pretty unconvinced anymore that you should bother even learning many of the hard sciences and maths that we used to as underpinnings. Like I used to believe that the right thing to do was for everybody to go into engineering. I'm not necessarily as convinced as I used to be, because I used to say, 'Well, that's great, first principles thinking, etc., etc., and you're going to get trained in a toolkit that will scale.' And I'm not sure that that's true. I think like you can use these agents and you can use deep research and all of a sudden they replace a lot of that skill. So what's left over? It's creativity, it's judgment, it's history, it's psychology, it's all of these other sort of soft skills, leadership, communication that allow you to manipulate these models in constructive ways. Because when you think of the prompt engineering that gets you to great answers, it's actually just thinking in totally different orthogonal ways and nonlinearly. So that's my last thought, which is it does open up the aperture, meaning for every smart mathematical genius, there's many, many, many other people who have high EQ, and all of a sudden this tool actually takes the skill away from the person with just a high IQ and says if you have these other skills, now you can compete with me equally. And I think that that's liberating for a lot of people.
turns.zh: Chat，你想就此总结一下这个机遇/替代/混乱的话题吗？我之前就想说，但我现在越来越不相信你还应该费心去学习很多我们过去视为基础的硬科学和数学了。比如我过去认为每个人都应该去学工程。我现在不像以前那么确信了，因为我过去常说：‘嗯，这很好，第一性原理思维等等，你会接受一套可扩展的工具包训练。’但我不确定这是否正确。我认为你可以使用这些智能体，使用深度研究，它们突然就取代了很多那种技能。那么剩下的是什么？是创造力、判断力、历史、心理学，以及所有其他软技能、领导力、沟通能力，这些让你能够以建设性的方式操控这些模型。因为当你想到能让你得到出色答案的提示工程时，它实际上只是以完全不同的正交和非线性方式思考。所以这是我最后的想法：它确实打开了视野，意味着对于每一个聪明的数学天才，还有许许多多其他高情商的人，突然之间，这个工具把技能从只有高智商的人手中夺走，并说如果你有这些其他技能，现在你可以和我平等竞争。我认为这对很多人来说是一种解放。

turns.en: I'm in the camp of more opportunity. You know, I got to watch the movie industry a whole bunch when the digital cameras came out, and more people started making documentaries, more people started making independent film shorts, and then of course the YouTube Revolution, people started making videos on YouTube or podcasts like this. And if you look at what happened with the special effects industry as well, we need far fewer people to make a Star Wars movie, to make a Star Wars series, to make a Marvel series. As we've seen now, we can get The Mandalorian and Ahsoka and all these other series with smaller numbers of people, and they look better than obviously the original Star Wars series or even the prequels.
