So there's going to be so many more opportunities. We're now making more TV shows, more series, everything we wanted to see of every little character. That's the same thing that's happening in startups. I can't believe that there is an app now, Naval, called Slopes just for skiing, and there are 20 really good apps for just meditation, and there are 10 really good ones just for fasting. Like we're going down this long tail of opportunity, and there'll be plenty of million to 10 million businesses for you. You know, people learn to use these tools. I love how that's the thing that tips you over: which one you get an extra Marvel movie or an extra Star Wars show. So that tips you over. I think for a lot of people, it feels great that AI may take over the world, but I'm gonna get a next Star Wars movie, so I'm cool.
turns.zh: 我属于更多机会的阵营。你知道，我目睹了电影行业在数码相机出现时的巨大变化，更多人开始制作纪录片，更多人开始制作独立短片，然后当然是YouTube革命，人们开始在YouTube上制作视频或像这样的播客。如果你看看特效行业发生了什么，制作一部《星球大战》电影、一部《星球大战》系列、一部漫威系列所需的人手大大减少了。正如我们现在所见，我们可以用更少的人制作《曼达洛人》、《阿索卡》以及所有其他系列，而且它们看起来显然比原版《星球大战》系列甚至前传更好。所以会有更多的机会。我们现在制作更多的电视节目、更多的系列，每一个小角色我们想看的都有了。初创公司也在发生同样的事情。我简直不敢相信现在有一个叫Slopes的应用，专门用于滑雪，还有20个非常好的冥想应用，以及10个非常好的禁食应用。我们正在沿着这条长长的机会尾巴走下去，会有大量百万到千万美元级别的生意等着你。你知道，人们学会了使用这些工具。我喜欢这一点让你心动：你得到一部额外的漫威电影或一部额外的《星球大战》剧集。所以这让你心动。我认为对很多人来说，AI可能会接管世界，这感觉很好，但我会得到下一部《星球大战》电影，所以我没问题。

turns.en: Yeah, I mean, are you not entertained? Want a final point on this: look, I mean, given the choice between the two categories of techno-optimist and techno-pessimist, I'm definitely in the optimist camp. And I think we should be. But I think there's actually a third category that I would submit, which is...
turns.zh: 是啊，我是说，你不觉得有趣吗？最后一点：你看，在技术乐观主义和技术悲观主义这两类之间做选择，我绝对是乐观主义阵营的。我认为我们也应该如此。但我认为实际上还有第三类，我想提出的是……

turns.en: Techno-realism is the view that technology is going to happen. Trying to stop it is like ordering the tides to stop. If we don't do it, somebody else will—China will do it, or somebody else will. It's better for us to be in control of the technology, to be the leader, rather than passively waiting for it to happen to us. I think that's always true: it's better for businesses to be proactive and take the lead, disrupt themselves instead of waiting for someone else to do it. And I think it's better for countries. I did see a hint of this techno-realism idea in the Vice President's speech and his tweet: look, AI is going to happen, we might as well be the leader. If we don't, we could lose in a key category that has implications for national security, our economy, and many things. So that's not a world we want to live in. I think a lot of this debate is academic because whether you're an optimist or pessimist is just glass half empty or half full. The question is just: is it going to happen or not? And I think the answer is yes. So then we want to control it. Let's boil it down: there's not a tremendous amount of choice in this.
turns.zh: 技术现实主义认为技术一定会发生。试图阻止它就像命令潮汐停下一样。如果我们不做，别人就会做——中国会做，或者其他人会做。我们最好掌控技术，成为领导者，而不是被动等待它降临到我们头上。我认为这总是对的：企业最好主动出击，引领变革，自我颠覆，而不是等着别人来做。对国家来说也是如此。我在副总统的演讲和推文中看到了技术现实主义的影子：看，人工智能一定会发生，我们不如成为领导者。如果我们不这样做，我们可能会在一个对国家安全、经济和许多方面都有影响的关键领域失利。所以我们不想生活在那样一个世界里。我认为这场辩论很多是学术性的，因为无论你是乐观主义者还是悲观主义者，都只是半杯水的问题。问题只是：它会不会发生？我认为答案是肯定的。那么我们就想控制它。简单来说：我们在这个问题上没有太多选择。

turns.en: I would agree heavily with one point and tweak another. The point I agree with is that it's going to happen anyway, and that's what DeepSeek proved. You can turn off the flow of chips to them and the flow of talent, but what do they do? They just get more efficient, and they exported it back to us. They sent us back the best open-source model when our guys were staying closed-source for safety reasons. DeepSeek exploded the fallacy that the US has a monopoly in this category and that somehow we can slow down the train and have total control over it. What DeepSeek showed is: no, if we slow down the train, they're just going to win.
turns.zh: 我非常同意一点，但想对另一点稍作调整。我同意的一点是，它无论如何都会发生，而 DeepSeek 证明了这一点。你可以切断对他们的芯片供应和人才流动，但他们怎么做？他们只是变得更高效，然后把它出口回给我们。当我们的团队出于安全原因保持闭源时，他们给我们送回了最好的开源模型。DeepSeek 打破了美国在这一领域拥有垄断地位、我们可以放慢列车速度并完全控制它的谬论。DeepSeek 表明：不，如果我们放慢列车速度，他们只会赢。

turns.en: The part I would tweak a little bit is the idea that we are going to win by 'we' when you say America. The problem is that the best way to win is to be as open, as distributed, as innovative as possible. If this all ends up in the control of one company, they're actually going to be slower to innovate than if there's a dynamic system. And that dynamic system by its nature will be open; it will leak to China, it will leak to India. But these things have powerful network effects. We know this about technology: almost all technologies have network effects underneath. So even if you are open, you're still going to win and you're still going to control. Look at the internet: that was all true for the internet, right? The internet is an open technology based on tons of open source. Who are the dominant companies? All the dominant companies are US companies because they were in the lead.
turns.zh: 我要稍作调整的一点是，当你说“我们”美国会赢时，问题在于，获胜的最佳方式是尽可能开放、分布、创新。如果这一切最终由一家公司控制，他们的创新速度实际上会比一个动态系统慢。而动态系统本质上将是开放的；它会泄露到中国，泄露到印度。但这些事物具有强大的网络效应。我们对技术了解这一点：几乎所有技术底层都有网络效应。所以即使你开放，你仍然会赢，仍然会控制。看看互联网：互联网就是如此，对吧？互联网是一种基于大量开源技术的开放技术。主导公司是谁？所有主导公司都是美国公司，因为它们处于领先地位。

turns.en: Exactly right. We embraced the open internet. That was different.
turns.zh: 完全正确。我们拥抱了开放的互联网。那是不一样的。

turns.en: So there will be benefits for all of humanity. And I think the Vice President's speech was really clear: look, we want you guys to be on board, we want to be good partners. However, there are definitely going to be winners economically and militarily. And in order to be one of those winners, you have to be a leader who's going to get to AGI first.
turns.zh: 所以这对全人类都有好处。我认为副总统的演讲非常明确：看，我们希望你们加入，我们希望成为好伙伴。然而，经济上和军事上肯定会有赢家。而要成为赢家之一，你必须成为率先实现 AGI 的领导者。

turns.en: Naval, is it going to be open source or closed source that wins the day? If we're sitting here five or ten years from now and looking at the top three language models... I don't think we know how to build AGI, but that's a much longer discussion. Who's going to have the best model five years from now?
turns.zh: Naval，最终会是开源还是闭源获胜？如果我们五年或十年后坐在这里，看看排名前三的语言模型……我不认为我们知道如何构建 AGI，但那是更长的讨论。五年后谁将拥有最好的模型？

turns.en: Hold on, I 100% agree with you. I just think it's a different thing. But what we're building are these incredible natural language computers. And actually, David in a very pithy way summarized the two big use cases: it's search and it's homework, it's paperwork. It's really paperwork, and a lot of these jobs that we're talking about disappearing are actually paperwork jobs. They're paperwork shuffling. These are made-up jobs. Like the federal government, as we're finding out through DOGE, a third of it is like people digging holes with spoons and another third are filling them back up. They're filling up paperwork and then burying it in a mineshaft. They're burying it in a mineshaft mountain.
turns.zh: 等等，我百分之百同意你。我只是认为这是不同的事情。但我们正在构建的是这些不可思议的自然语言计算机。实际上，David 非常简洁地总结了两个主要用例：搜索和家庭作业，以及文书工作。这真的是文书工作，我们谈论的许多即将消失的工作实际上都是文书工作。它们是文书搬运。这些是虚构的工作。就像联邦政府，我们通过 DOGE 发现，三分之一的人在用勺子挖洞，另外三分之一在填洞。他们填满文书工作，然后把它埋进矿井里。他们把它埋进矿井山。

turns.en: Yeah, I think a lot of these made-up jobs—they got to go down the mineshaft to get the paperwork when someone retires and bring it up. You know what? I'm going to get them some thumb drives. We can increase the throughput of the elevator with some thumb drives. It would be incredible. What we found out is the DMV has been running the government for the last 70 years. It's been compounding. That's really what's going on. DMV is in charge. I mean, if the world ends in nuclear war, God forbid, the only things left will be the cockroaches and then a bunch of government work documents, TPS reports, down in the mineshaft basically.
turns.zh: 是的，我认为很多这些虚构的工作——他们得下到矿井里，当有人退休时取出文书工作再带上来。你知道吗？我要给他们一些 U 盘。我们可以用 U 盘提高电梯的吞吐量。那会不可思议。我们发现 DMV 在过去 70 年里一直在管理政府。这是复利效应。这就是实际情况。DMV 在负责。我的意思是，如果世界在核战争中终结，上帝保佑，唯一剩下的将是蟑螂，然后是一堆政府工作文件、TPS 报告，基本上都在矿井里。

turns.en: Yeah, let's take a moment everybody to thank our Zar. We miss him. We wish he could be here for the whole show. And thank you, Zar. Thank you to the Zar. See you guys. Miss you. We miss you, little buddy. I wish we could talk about Ukraine, but we're not allowed. Get back to work. We'll talk about it another time over coffee. I'll see you in the commissary. Thanks for the invite. Bye, man.
turns.zh: 是的，让我们花点时间感谢我们的 Zar。我们想念他。我们希望他能全程参与节目。谢谢你，Zar。谢谢 Zar。再见，各位。想你。我们想你，小家伙。我希望我们能谈谈乌克兰，但不允许。回去工作吧。我们改天喝咖啡时再聊。我会在食堂见你。谢谢邀请。再见，伙计。

turns.en: I'm so excited. Naval invited me to go to the military mess. I'm going to be in the commissary. No, he didn't. You invited yourself. Be honest.
turns.zh: 我太兴奋了。Naval 邀请我去军队食堂。我要去小卖部了。不，他没有。是你自己邀请自己的。老实说。

turns.en: I did, yes I did. I put it on his calendar.
turns.zh: 是的，我确实邀请了。我把它放在他的日历上了。

turns.en: To keep the conversation moving, let me segue into a point that came up that was really important: tariffs. And the point is, even though the internet was open, the US won a lot of the internet. A lot of US companies won the internet, and they won that because we got there the firstest with the mostest, as they say in the military. And that matters because a lot of technology businesses have scale economies and network effects underneath, even basic brand-based network effects. If you go back to the late 90s, early 2000s, very few people would have predicted that we would have ended up with Amazon basically owning all of e-commerce. You would have thought it would have been perfect competition and very spread out. And that applies to how we end up with Uber as basically one taxi service, or we end up with Airbnb, Meta. It's just network effects, network effects, network effects rule the world. But when it comes to tariffs and when it comes to trade, we act like network effects don't exist. The classic Ricardian comparative advantage dogma says that you should produce what you're best at, I produce what I'm best at, and we trade. And then even if you want to charge me more for it, if you want to impose tariffs for me to ship to you, I should still...
turns.zh: 为了保持对话进行，让我转入一个非常重要的话题：关税。关键是，尽管互联网是开放的，美国赢得了互联网的很大一部分。许多美国公司赢得了互联网，他们之所以赢，是因为我们以军事术语所说的“最先最多”到达。这很重要，因为许多科技企业底层具有规模经济和网络效应，甚至基本的基于品牌的网络效应。如果你回到 90 年代末、2000 年代初，很少有人会预测到亚马逊最终会基本上拥有所有电子商务。你会认为那会是完全竞争且非常分散的。这同样适用于我们最终如何得到 Uber 作为基本上一家出租车服务，或者我们得到 Airbnb、Meta。只是网络效应、网络效应、网络效应统治世界。但当涉及到关税和贸易时，我们表现得好像网络效应不存在。经典的李嘉图比较优势教条说，你应该生产你最擅长的，我生产我最擅长的，然后我们贸易。然后即使你想对我收取更多费用，如果你想对我运给你的商品征收关税，我仍然应该……

transcript.secZh: 技术现实主义与AI的必然性

turns.en: Keep tariffs down because I'm better off you're just selling me stuff cheaply great or if you want to subsidize your guys great you're selling me stuff cheaply. The problem is that is not how most modern businesses work. Most modern businesses have network effects. As a simple thought experiment, suppose that we have two countries right I'm China you're the US. I start out by subsidizing all of my companies and industries that have network effects. So I'll subsidize TikTok, I'll ban your social media but I'll push mine. I will subsidize my semiconductors which tend to have winner-take-all in certain categories, or I'll subsidize my drones, and then eventually BYD, self-driving, whatever. And then when I win, I own the whole market and I can raise prices. And if you try to start up a competitor, then it's too late. I've got network effects, or if I've got scale economies, I can lower my price to zero, crash you out of business, no one in their right mind will invest, and I'll raise prices right back up. So you have to understand that certain industries have hysteresis or they have network effects or they have economies of scale, and these are all the interesting ones, these are all the high margin businesses. So in those, if somebody is subsidizing or they're raising tariffs against you to protect your industries and let them develop, you do have to do something. You can't just completely back down. What are your thoughts, Jamanth, about tariffs and network effects? It does seem like we do want to have redundancy in supply chain, so there are some exceptions here. Any thoughts on how this might play out? Because yeah, Trump brings up tariffs every 48 hours and then it doesn't seem like any of them land, so I don't know. I'm still on my 72-hour Trump rule, which is whatever he says, wait 72 hours and then maybe see if it actually comes to pass. Where do you stand on all these tariffs and tariff talk?
turns.zh: 保持低关税，因为这样我更有利，你只是便宜卖给我东西，很好。或者如果你想补贴你的人，那也很好，你还是在便宜卖给我东西。但问题是，这不是大多数现代企业的运作方式。大多数现代企业都有网络效应。举个简单的思想实验：假设有两个国家，我是中国，你是美国。我一开始补贴所有具有网络效应的公司和行业。所以我补贴TikTok，我禁止你的社交媒体，但推广我的。我补贴我的半导体，这些半导体在某些类别中往往赢家通吃，或者我补贴我的无人机，然后比亚迪、自动驾驶等等。当我赢了，我就拥有整个市场，我可以提高价格。如果你试图创办一个竞争对手，那就太晚了。我有网络效应，或者如果我有规模经济，我可以把价格降到零，把你挤出市场，没有人会理智地投资，然后我再把价格涨回来。所以你必须明白，某些行业有滞后效应，或者有网络效应，或者有规模经济，这些都是有趣的行业，都是高利润的业务。在这些行业中，如果有人补贴或对你加征关税来保护他们的行业并让其发展，你必须采取行动。你不能完全退缩。Jamanth，你对关税和网络效应有什么看法？我们确实希望供应链有冗余，所以这里有一些例外。你对这将如何发展有什么想法？因为特朗普每48小时就提关税，但似乎没有一个落地，所以我不知道。我仍然坚持我的72小时特朗普规则：不管他说什么，等72小时，然后看看是否真的发生。你对所有这些关税和关税言论持什么立场？

turns.en: Well, I think the tariffs will be a thing. Are they coming? Absolutely. The quantum of them I don't know. And I think that the way that you can figure out how extreme it will be, it'll be based on what the legislative plan is for the budget. So there are two paths right now. Path one, which I think is a little bit more likely, is that they're going to pass a slimmed-down plan in the Senate just on border security and military spending, and then they'll kick the can down the road for probably another three or four months on the budget. Path two is this one big beautiful bill that's working its way through the House, and there they're proposing trillions of dollars of cuts. In that mode, you're going to need to raise revenue somehow, and especially if you're giving away tax breaks, the only way to do that is probably through tariffs, or one way to do it is through tariffs. My honest opinion, Jason, is that I think we're in a very complicated moment. I think the Senate plan is actually on the margins more likely and better, and the reason is because I think that Trump is better off getting the next 60 to 90 days of data. I mean, we're in a real pickle here. We have persistent inflation, we have a broken Fed, they are totally asleep at the switch, and the thing that Yellen and Biden did, which in hindsight now was extremely dangerous, is they issued so much short-term paper that in totality we have $1 trillion that we need to finance in the next 6 to 9 months. So it could be the case that we have rates that are like five, five and a quarter, five and a half percent. I think that that's extremely bad at the same time as inflation, at the same time as delinquencies are ticking up. So I think tariffs are probably going to happen, but I think that Trump will have the most flexibility if he has time to see what the actual economic conditions will be, which will be more clear in three, four, five months. And so I almost think this big beautiful bill is actually counterproductive because I'm not sure we're gonna have all the data we need to get it right.
turns.zh: 嗯，我认为关税会是一个问题。它们会来吗？绝对会。具体幅度我不知道。我认为你可以通过预算的立法计划来判断它会有多极端。现在有两条路径。路径一，我认为可能性稍大一点，是参议院通过一个精简的计划，只涉及边境安全和军费开支，然后预算问题再推迟三到四个月。路径二是这个正在众议院推进的“一大美丽法案”，他们提议削减数万亿美元。在这种模式下，你需要以某种方式增加收入，尤其是如果你要减税，唯一的方法可能就是通过关税，或者说一种方法是通过关税。我的诚实意见，Jason，是我们处在一个非常复杂的时刻。我认为参议院的计划实际上更可能也更好，原因是特朗普最好先获得接下来60到90天的数据。我的意思是，我们真的陷入了困境。我们有持续的通货膨胀，美联储失灵，他们完全失职，而耶伦和拜登做的事情，事后看来极其危险，是他们发行了太多短期债券，总计我们需要在未来6到9个月内融资1万亿美元。所以利率可能会达到5%、5.25%、5.5%。我认为这非常糟糕，同时还有通胀，同时违约率在上升。所以我认为关税很可能会发生，但我认为如果特朗普有时间看看实际经济状况会怎样，那他将拥有最大的灵活性，而经济状况在三、四、五个月后会更加清晰。所以我几乎认为这个“一大美丽法案”实际上适得其反，因为我不确定我们是否会有所有必要的数据来把它做对。

turns.en: Fred, any thoughts on these tariffs? You've been involved in the global marketplace, especially when it comes to produce and wheat and all this corn and everything. What do you think the dynamic here is going to be? Or is it saber rattling and a tool for Trump?
turns.zh: Fred，你对这些关税有什么看法？你一直参与全球市场，尤其是在农产品、小麦、玉米等方面。你认为这里的动态会是什么？还是说这只是虚张声势，是特朗普的一个工具？

turns.en: The biggest buyer of US agricultural exports is China. Chinese agricultural exports are a major revenue source, major income source, and a major part of the economy for a large number of states. And so there will be, as there was in the first Trump presidency, very likely very large transfer payments made to farmers because China is very likely going to tariff imports or stop making import purchases altogether, which is what happened during the first presidency. When they did that, the federal government, I believe, had transfer payments of north of $20 billion to farmers. This is a not negligible sum, and it's a not negligible economic effect because there's then a rippling effect throughout the agricultural economy. So I think that's one key thing that I've heard folks talk about is the activity that's going to be needed to support the farm economy as the US's biggest agricultural customer disappears.
turns.zh: 美国农产品出口的最大买家是中国。中国的农产品出口是许多州的主要收入来源、主要收入来源和经济的重要组成部分。因此，就像在特朗普第一个总统任期内一样，很可能会有非常大的转移支付给农民，因为中国很可能会对进口征收关税或完全停止进口采购，这正是第一个总统任期内发生的事情。当他们这样做时，我相信联邦政府向农民提供了超过200亿美元的转移支付。这不是一个小数目，也不是一个可以忽略的经济影响，因为随后整个农业经济会产生连锁反应。所以我认为我听到人们谈论的一个关键点是，随着美国最大的农业客户消失，需要采取什么行动来支持农业经济。

turns.en: In the early 20th century, we didn't have an income tax and the federal revenue was almost entirely dependent on tariffs. When tariffs were cut, there was an expectation that there would be a decline in federal government revenue, but what actually happened is volume went up. So lower tariffs actually increased trade, increased the size of the economies. This is where a lot of economists take their basis in: hey guys, if we do these tariffs, it's actually going to shrink the economy, it's going to cause a reduction in trade. The counterbalancing effect is one that has not been tested in economics, right, which is what's going to happen if simultaneously we reduce the income tax and reduce the corporate income tax and basically increased capital flows through reduced taxation while doing the tariff implementation at the same time. So it's a grand economic experiment, and I think we'll learn a lot about what's going to happen here as this all moves forward. I do think ultimately these countries are going to capitulate to some degree and we're going to end up with some negotiated settlement that's going to hopefully not be too short-term impactful on the economies and the people and the jobs that are dependent on trade. Economy feels like it's in a very precarious place. It does to asset holders obviously, and obviously they've left it in a bad place in the last Administration and we shut down the entire country for a year over COVID. The bill for that has come due and that's reflected in inflation. I think there are a couple other points on tariffs. First is, it's not just about money, it's also about making sure we have a functional middle class with good jobs. Because if you have a non-tariff world, maybe all the gains go to the upper class and an underclass, and then you can't have a functioning democracy when the average person is on one of those two extremes. So I think that's one issue. Another is strategic industries. If you look at it today, probably the...
turns.zh: 在20世纪初，我们没有所得税，联邦收入几乎完全依赖关税。当关税被削减时，人们预期联邦政府收入会下降，但实际发生的是贸易量上升了。所以较低的关税实际上增加了贸易，扩大了经济规模。这就是许多经济学家的依据：如果我们征收这些关税，实际上会收缩经济，导致贸易减少。但抵消效应在经济学中尚未被检验，即如果我们在实施关税的同时，减少所得税和公司税，通过减税增加资本流动，会发生什么。所以这是一个宏大的经济实验，我认为随着这一切的推进，我们会学到很多。我确实认为最终这些国家会在一定程度上屈服，我们会达成某种谈判解决方案，希望不会对依赖贸易的经济、人民和就业产生太短期的影响。经济感觉处于非常不稳定的状态。对资产持有者来说显然如此，而且上一届政府把它留在了糟糕的状态，我们因为新冠疫情关闭了整个国家一年。那笔账单已经到期，反映在通货膨胀中。我认为关于关税还有几点。首先，这不仅仅是钱的问题，还关乎确保我们有一个拥有好工作的功能性中产阶级。因为如果是一个没有关税的世界，也许所有收益都流向上层阶级和下层阶级，那么当普通人处于这两个极端之一时，你无法拥有一个运作良好的民主制度。所以我认为这是一个问题。另一个是战略产业。如果你看看今天，可能……

transcript.sec: Drone Supply Chain and National Security

turns.en: The largest defense contractor in the world is DJI. They got all the drones. Even in Ukraine, both sides are getting all their drone parts from DJI. Now they're getting it through different supply chains and so on, but Ukrainian drones and Russian drones, the vast majority of them are coming through China, through DJI. And we don't have that industry. If we have a kinetic conflict right now and we don't have a good drone supply chain internally in the US, we're probably going to lose because those things are autonomous bullets. That's the feature of all warfare. We're buying F-35s, and the Chinese are building swarms of drones at scale. So we do have to re-on-shore those critical supply chains. And what is a drone supply chain? It's not just a thing called a drone; it's motors, semiconductors, optics, lasers, just everything across the board. So I think there are other good arguments for at least reshoring some of these industries. We need them. And the United States is very lucky in that it's very autarkic. We have all the resources, we have all the supplies. We can be upstream of everybody with all the energy. To the extent we're importing any energy, that is a choice we made, not because fundamentally we lack the energy. Because between all the oil resources, natural gas, fracking, combined with all the work we've done in nuclear fission and small reactors, we should absolutely be energy independent. We should be running the table on it. We should have a massive surplus. And hey, if you're worried about a couple million DoorDash Uber drivers losing their jobs to automation, hey, there's going to be factories to build these parts for these drones that we're going to need. So there's a lot of opportunity, I guess, for people. And there is a difference between different kinds of jobs. Those kinds of jobs are better jobs—building difficult things at scale physically that we need for both national security and for innovation. Those are better jobs than paperwork, writing essays for other people to read, or even driving cars.
turns.zh: 全球最大的防务承包商是大疆。他们垄断了无人机市场。即使在乌克兰，双方都在从大疆获取无人机零件，只是通过不同的供应链。但乌克兰和俄罗斯的无人机，绝大多数都来自中国，来自大疆。而我们没有这个产业。如果我们现在发生热战，而美国国内没有良好的无人机供应链，我们很可能会输，因为那些东西就是自主子弹。这就是所有战争的特征。我们在买F-35，而中国人在大规模制造无人机蜂群。所以我们确实需要重新本土化那些关键供应链。什么是无人机供应链？它不只是一个叫无人机的东西，而是电机、半导体、光学、激光，方方面面。所以我认为至少有一些其他好的理由来重新本土化这些产业。我们需要它们。美国非常幸运，因为它几乎是自给自足的。我们拥有所有资源，所有供应。我们可以凭借所有能源成为所有人的上游。我们进口任何能源都是我们自己的选择，而不是因为我们根本上缺乏能源。因为结合所有石油资源、天然气、水力压裂，再加上我们在核裂变和小型反应堆方面所做的工作，我们绝对应该实现能源独立。我们应该全面领先。我们应该有巨大的盈余。嘿，如果你担心几百万DoorDash和Uber司机因自动化而失业，嘿，会有工厂来制造我们需要的无人机零件。所以我认为人们有很多机会。不同类型的工作之间是有区别的。那些工作是更好的工作——大规模物理制造我们为国家安全和创新所需的困难东西。那些工作比文书工作、为别人写文章，甚至开车要好。

transcript.secZh: 无人机供应链与国家安全

transcript.sec: Copyright Case: Thomson Reuters vs Ross

turns.en: All right, listen, I want to get to two more stories here. We have a really interesting copyright story that I wanted to touch on. Thomson Reuters just won the first major US AI copyright case, and fair use played a major role in this decision. This has huge implications for AI companies here in the United States. Obviously, OpenAI and the New York Times, Getty Images versus Stability—we've talked about these, but it's been a little while because the legal system takes a little bit of time, and these are very complicated cases as we've talked about. Thomson Reuters owns Westlaw, if you don't know. It's kind of like LexisNexis, it's one of the legal databases out there that lawyers use to find cases, etc. And they have a paid product with summaries and analysis of legal decisions. Back in 2020, this is two years before ChatGPT, Reuters sued a legal research competitor called Ross for copyright infringement. Ross had created an AI-powered legal search engine—sounds great—but Ross had asked Westlaw if they would pay a license to its content for training. Westlaw said no. This all went back and forth, and then Ross signed a similar deal with a company called LegalEase. The problem is LegalEase's database was just copied and pasted from a bunch of Westlaw answers. So Reuters/Westlaw sued Ross in 2020, accusing the company of being vicariously liable for LegalEase's direct infringement. Super important point. Anyway, the judge originally favored Ross on fair use. This week, the judge reversed this ruling and found Ross liable, noting that after further review, fair use does not apply in this case. This is the first major win. And we debated this, so here's a clip: you know, you heard it here first on the All-In Pod. What I would say is, you know, when you look at that fair use doctrine, I've got a lot of experience with it. You know the fourth factor test—I'm sure you're well aware of this—is the effect of the use on the potential market and the value of the work. If you look at the lawsuits that are starting to emerge, it is Getty's right to then make derivative products based on their images. I think we would all agree: Stable Diffusion, when they use the open web, that is no excuse to use an open web crawler to avoid getting a license from the original owner of that. Just because you can technically do it doesn't mean you're allowed to do it. In fact, the open web projects that provide these say explicitly: we do not give you the right to use this; you have to then go read the copyright laws on each of those websites. And on top of that, if somebody were to steal the copyrights of other people and put it on the open web—which is happening all day long—you still, if you're building a derivative work like this, you still need to go get it. So it's no excuse that I took some site in Russia that did a bunch of copyright violation and then I indexed them for my training model. So I think this is going to result in... free? Can you shoot me in the face and let me know when this is over? Oh, great. I feel the same way. Same way. Now exactly. I know, me too. Yeah, okay, good, good segment. Let's move on. Well, since these guys don't give a damn about copyright holders, what do you think about...?
turns.zh: 好了，听着，我想再讲两个故事。我们有一个非常有趣的版权故事，我想提一下。汤森路透刚刚赢得了美国首例重大AI版权案，合理使用在这项判决中发挥了重要作用。这对美国的AI公司有巨大影响。显然，OpenAI和《纽约时报》、Getty Images对Stability——我们谈过这些，但已经有一段时间了，因为法律系统需要时间，而且这些案件非常复杂，正如我们讨论过的。汤森路透拥有Westlaw，如果你不知道的话。它有点像LexisNexis，是律师用来查找案例等的法律数据库之一。他们有一个付费产品，提供法律判决的摘要和分析。早在2020年，也就是ChatGPT问世前两年，路透社起诉了一家名为Ross的法律研究竞争对手，指控其侵犯版权。Ross创建了一个AI驱动的法律搜索引擎——听起来很棒——但Ross曾询问Westlaw是否愿意付费许可其内容用于训练。Westlaw拒绝了。这件事来回折腾，然后Ross与一家名为LegalEase的公司签署了类似协议。问题是LegalEase的数据库只是从一堆Westlaw答案中复制粘贴过来的。所以路透社/Westlaw在2020年起诉Ross，指控该公司对LegalEase的直接侵权承担替代责任。非常重要的一点。无论如何，法官最初在合理使用问题上支持Ross。本周，法官推翻了这一裁决，认定Ross负有责任，并指出经过进一步审查，合理使用不适用于此案。这是第一个重大胜利。我们对此进行过辩论，所以这里有一段片段：你知道，你是在All-In播客上最先听到的。我想说的是，当你审视合理使用原则时，我对此有丰富的经验。你知道第四个因素测试——我相信你很熟悉——是使用对潜在市场和工作价值的影响。如果你看看那些开始出现的诉讼，Getty有权基于其图像制作衍生作品。我想我们都会同意：Stable Diffusion，当他们使用开放网络时，这不能成为使用开放网络爬虫来避免从原始所有者那里获得许可的借口。仅仅因为你在技术上可以做到，并不意味着你被允许这样做。事实上，提供这些的开放网络项目明确表示：我们不授予你使用它的权利；你必须去阅读每个网站上的版权法。除此之外，如果有人窃取他人的版权并将其放在开放网络上——这每天都在发生——如果你正在构建这样的衍生作品，你仍然需要去获取许可。所以，我拿了一个俄罗斯的网站，它做了大量版权侵权，然后我将其索引用于我的训练模型，这不是借口。所以我认为这将导致……自由？你能朝我脸上开枪，然后告诉我什么时候结束吗？哦，太好了。我也有同感。同感。现在确实如此。我知道，我也是。是的，好的，好的片段。我们继续吧。好吧，既然这些家伙不关心版权持有人，你怎么看……？

turns.en: I'm so glad you're here, Naval, to actually talk about the topics. These two other guys, I'm going to go out on a limb. I'm going to go out on an even thinner limb and say I largely agree with you. I think it's a bit rich to crawl the open web, hoover up all the data, offer direct substitution for a lot of use cases because, you know, now you start and end with the AI model. It's not even like you link out like Google did, and then you just close off the models for safety reasons. I think if you trained on the open web, your model should be open source. Yeah, absolutely. That would be a fine thing. I have a prediction here. I think this is all going to wind up like the Napster or Spotify case. For people who don't know, Spotify pays, I think, 65 cents on the dollar to the original underwriters of that content, the music industry, and they figured out a way to make a business, and Napster is roadkill. I think that there is a nonzero chance—like it might be 5 or 10%—that OpenAI is going to lose the New York Times lawsuit, and they're going to lose it hard, and there are going to be injunctions. And I think the settlement might be that these language models, especially the closed ones, are going to have to pay some percentage in a negotiated settlement of their revenue—half, two-thirds—to the content holders. And this could make the content industry have a massive, massive uplift and a massive resurgence. I think the problem is there's an example on the other side of this, which is that there's a company that provides technical support for Oracle, a third-party company, and Oracle has tried multiple times to sue them into oblivion using copyright infringement as part of the...
turns.zh: 我很高兴你在这里，Naval，来真正讨论这些话题。这两个家伙，我要冒个险。我要冒更大的险，说我基本同意你的看法。我认为爬取开放网络、吸走所有数据、为许多用例提供直接替代品，这有点过分，因为你知道，现在你以AI模型开始和结束。它甚至不像谷歌那样链接出去，然后你仅仅出于安全原因封闭模型。我认为如果你在开放网络上训练，你的模型应该是开源的。是的，绝对。那会是一件好事。我有一个预测。我认为这一切最终会像Napster或Spotify案一样。对于不知道的人来说，Spotify支付，我认为，每美元65美分给内容的原始承销商，音乐行业，他们找到了做生意的办法，而Napster成了路毙动物。我认为存在非零概率——可能是5%或10%——OpenAI会输掉《纽约时报》的诉讼，而且会输得很惨，还会有禁令。我认为和解可能是这些语言模型，尤其是封闭模型，将不得不支付其收入的一定比例——一半、三分之二——通过协商和解给内容持有人。这可能会使内容行业获得巨大的、巨大的提升和巨大的复兴。我认为问题在于另一边有一个例子，那就是有一家公司为Oracle提供技术支持，是一家第三方公司，Oracle曾多次试图用版权侵权作为部分理由起诉他们，让他们消失……

turns.en: Justification and it's been a PA over the stock for a long time. The company's name is Riny Street. Don't ask me why, it's on my radar but I've been looking at it. They lost this huge lawsuit Oracle won, and then it went to appellate court and then it was all vacated. Why am I bringing this up? I think that the legal community has absolutely no idea how these models work, because you can find one case that goes one way and one case that goes the other. What I would say should become standard reading for anybody bringing in any of these lawsuits: there's an incredible video that Karpathy just dropped, that Andrej just dropped, where he does like this deep dive into LLMs and he explains ChatGPT from the ground up. It's on YouTube, it's three hours, it's excellent, and it's very difficult to watch that and not get to the same conclusion that you guys did. I'll just leave it at that.
turns.zh: 我长期关注一只股票，公司名叫Riny Street。别问我为什么，它一直在我的雷达上。他们输了一场大官司，Oracle赢了，然后上诉法院又全部撤销了。我为什么提这个？我认为法律界对这些模型的工作原理一无所知，因为你可以找到一个案子判这边赢，另一个案子判那边赢。我觉得任何提起这类诉讼的人都应该把一段视频作为必读材料——Karpathy刚发布了一个关于LLM的深度解析，从头讲解ChatGPT。在YouTube上，三个小时，非常精彩。看完那段视频很难不得出和你们一样的结论。我就说这么多。

turns.en: I tend to agree with this. There's also a good old video by Ilya Sutskever where he was, I believe, the founding Chief Scientist or CTO of OpenAI, and he talks about how these large language models are basically extreme compressors. He models them entirely as their ability to compress, and they're lossy compression.
turns.zh: 我倾向于同意这一点。还有一段Ilya Sutskever的经典视频，他应该是OpenAI的创始首席科学家或CTO，他谈到这些大语言模型本质上是极端的压缩器。他完全用压缩能力来建模它们，而且是有损压缩。

turns.en: Exactly, lossy compression.
turns.zh: 没错，有损压缩。

turns.en: Exactly. And Google got sued for fair use back in the day, but the way they managed to get past the argument was they were always linking back to you, they showed tiny snippets, they sent you the traffic. This is lossy compression. It is absolutely... I'm now on your side. I hate to say this, Jason, I agree with you. You were right, you were right.
turns.zh: 没错。当年Google因为合理使用被起诉，但他们绕过这个论点的方式是始终链接回你，显示小片段，把流量导给你。这是有损压缩。我现在站在你这边。我讨厌这么说，Jason，我同意你。你是对的，你是对的。

turns.en: That's all I wanted to hear. All these years, that's all I wanted. Shaking my head when I saw those videos because I was like, oh man, Jason was right. Jason was right. Oh my God.
turns.zh: 这就是我想听到的。这么多年，这就是我想要的。看到那些视频时我直摇头，因为我想，天哪，Jason是对的。Jason是对的。哦我的天。

turns.en: No, I just... I've been through this so many times. I think this is... you know, Rupert Murdoch said we should hold the line with Google and not allow them to index our content without a license, and Google navigated it successfully and they were able to not get him to stop. I think what's happened now is that the New York Times remembers that they all remember losing their content and these snippets and the one box to Google, and they couldn't get that genie back in the bottle. I think the New York Times realizes this is their payday. I think the New York Times will make more money from licenses from LLMs than they will make from advertising or subscriptions. Eventually this will renew the model almost. I think the New York Times content is worthless to an LLM, but that's a different story.
turns.zh: 不，我只是……我经历过太多次了。我认为……你知道，鲁珀特·默多克说过我们应该对Google保持强硬，不允许他们在没有许可的情况下索引我们的内容，但Google成功应对了，没有让他得逞。我认为现在发生的是，《纽约时报》记得他们当年在片段和OneBox上输给了Google，而且无法让那个精灵回到瓶子里。我认为《纽约时报》意识到这是他们的发财机会。我认为《纽约时报》从LLM许可中赚的钱会比从广告或订阅中赚的更多。最终这几乎会重塑商业模式。我认为《纽约时报》的内容对LLM来说毫无价值，但那是另一回事。

turns.en: I think the actual value... political reason, whatever. But I can tell you as a user, I loved The Wirecutter. I think you knew Brian and everybody over at The Wirecutter. That was like fair enough. Yeah, Wirecutter, what a great product. I used to pay for the New York Times. I no longer pay for the New York Times. My main reason was I would go to The Wirecutter and I would just buy whatever they told me to buy. Now I go to ChatGPT, which I pay for, it tells me what to buy based on The Wirecutter. So it's... and I'm already paying for it, so I stop paying for it.
turns.zh: 我认为实际价值……政治原因，随便吧。但我可以作为一个用户告诉你，我喜欢The Wirecutter。我想你认识Brian和Wirecutter的所有人。那很公平。是啊，Wirecutter，多好的产品。我以前付费订阅《纽约时报》，现在不付了。主要原因是以前我去Wirecutter，他们让我买什么我就买什么。现在我去ChatGPT，我付钱给它，它根据Wirecutter告诉我买什么。所以……我已经在付钱了，所以我不再付给《纽约时报》了。

turns.en: I philosophically disagree with all of your nonsense on this topic. All three of you are wrong, and I'll tell you why. Number one: if information is out in the open internet, I believe it's accessible and it's viewable, and I view an LLM or a web crawler as basically being a human that's reading and can store information in its brain. If it's out there in the open, if it's behind a paywall, 100%. If it's behind some protected password... wait, wait, wait, David. In that case, can a Google crawler just crawl an entire site and serve it on Google? Why can't they do that?
turns.zh: 我在哲学上不同意你们在这个话题上的所有废话。你们三个都错了，我告诉你们为什么。第一：如果信息在开放的互联网上，我认为它是可访问和可查看的，我把LLM或网络爬虫看作是一个正在阅读并能将信息存储在脑子里的人。如果它在开放的地方，如果它在付费墙后面，100%可以。如果它在受密码保护后面……等等，等等，David。那样的话，Google爬虫能爬取整个网站并在Google上提供吗？为什么它们不能那样做？

turns.en: Here's the fair use: the fair use is you cannot copy, you cannot repeat the content, you cannot take the content and repeat it. That is how the law is currently written. But now what I have is a tool that can remix it with 50 other pieces of similar content, and I can change the words slightly and maybe even translate into a different language. So where does it stop? Do you know the musical artist Girl Talk? We should have done a Girl Talk track here today. He's got musical taste, this guy. Oh good, here we go. He basically takes small samples of popular tracks and he made... and he got sued for the same problem. There was another guy named White Panda, I believe, had the same problem. Ed Sheeran got sued for this. Yeah. But their entire sites like Stack Overflow and Wikipedia have basically disappeared now because you can just swallow them all up and you can just spit it all back out in ChatGPT with slight changes. So I think that the fair... how much of a slight change is exactly the right question. How much are you changing? Yeah. So that's the question. And it actually boils down to the AGI question: are these things actually intelligent and are they learning, or are they compressing and regurgitating? That's the question.
turns.zh: 合理使用是这样的：你不能复制，你不能重复内容，你不能拿内容然后重复它。这是目前法律的写法。但现在我有了一个工具，可以把内容和其他50个类似内容混在一起，我可以稍微改几个词，甚至翻译成另一种语言。那么界限在哪里？你知道音乐人Girl Talk吗？我们今天真该放一首他的歌。他很有音乐品味。他基本上从流行歌曲中取小样本，然后……他因为同样的问题被起诉了。还有一个人叫White Panda，也有同样的问题。Ed Sheeran也因为这个被起诉了。但像Stack Overflow和维基百科这样的整个网站现在基本上消失了，因为你可以把它们全部吞下，然后在ChatGPT里稍微改改就吐出来。所以我认为合理使用……改变多少才算是微小的改变，这正是问题所在。你改变了多少？是啊。所以这就是问题。这实际上归结为AGI问题：这些东西是真的智能，在学习，还是在压缩和重复？这就是问题。

turns.en: I wonder this about humans. And that's why I bring up the White Panda, the Girl Talk in audio, but also visual art. There were always artists, and even in classical music... I don't know if you guys are classical music people, but there was a demonstration of how one composer learned from the next, and that you can actually track the music as kind of standing on the shoulders of the prior. And the same is true in almost all art forms, in almost all human knowledge and media communication. It's very hard to figure that out.
turns.zh: 我对人类也有同样的疑问。这就是为什么我提到White Panda、Girl Talk在音频领域，还有视觉艺术。总有艺术家，即使在古典音乐中……我不知道你们是不是古典音乐爱好者，但有一个演示说明一个作曲家如何从另一个作曲家学习，你实际上可以追踪音乐是站在前人的肩膀上。这在几乎所有艺术形式、几乎所有人类知识和媒体传播中都是如此。这很难搞清楚。

turns.en: Well, that's exactly right. That's the hard... it's very hard to figure that out. Which is why I come back to: there's only one of two stable solutions to this, and it's going to happen anyway. If we don't crawl it, the Chinese will crawl it. DeepSeek proved that. So there's only one of two stable solutions: either you pay the copyright holders, which I actually think doesn't work, and the reason is because someone in China will crawl it and they just dump the weights, right? So they can just crawl and compress and dump the compressed weights. Or if you crawl, make it open, at least contribute something back to open source. Right? You crawled open data, contributed back to open source, and the people who don't want to be crawled, they're going to have to go to a huge length to protect their data. Now everybody knows to protect the data.
turns.zh: 嗯，完全正确。这很难……这很难搞清楚。这就是为什么我回到：这个问题只有两种稳定的解决方案，而且无论如何都会发生。如果我们不爬取，中国人会爬取。DeepSeek证明了这一点。所以只有两种稳定的解决方案：要么你付钱给版权持有者，但我认为这行不通，因为中国会有人爬取然后直接转储权重，对吧？他们可以爬取、压缩并转储压缩后的权重。要么如果你爬取，就让它开放，至少为开源做点贡献。对吧？你爬取开放数据，回馈给开源，而那些不想被爬取的人，将不得不花大力气保护他们的数据。现在大家都知道要保护数据了。

turns.en: Yeah, well, thing is happening here. I have a book out from Harper Business on the shelf behind me, and I'm getting $2,500 for the next three years for Microsoft indexing it. So they're going out and they're licensing this stuff, and they're going $2,500. So your book literally... I'm getting $2,500 for three years from Harper to go into an LLM, to go into Microsoft specifically. And you know what? I'm going to sign it. I decided because I just want to set the precedent. Maybe next time it's $10,000, maybe next time it's $250. I don't care. I just want to see people have their content respected. And I'm just hoping that Sam Altman loses this lawsuit.
turns.zh: 是啊，事情正在发生。我身后架子上有一本Harper Business出版的书，我因为微软索引它，未来三年能拿到2500美元。所以他们出去授权这些东西，他们出价2500美元。所以你的书真的……我从Harper那里拿到2500美元，为期三年，让内容进入LLM，具体是进入微软。你知道吗？我会签的。我决定这么做是因为我想树立一个先例。也许下次是1万美元，也许下次是250美元。我不在乎。我只是希望看到人们的内容得到尊重。而且我只是希望Sam Altman输掉这场官司。

turns.en: And they get an injunction against it. Hey, well, just because he's such a weasel in terms of making OpenAI into a closed thing. I mean, I like Sam personally, but I think what he did was the super weasel move of all time for his own personal benefit. If he—and this whole lying like, 'Oh, I have no equity, I get healthcare'—no, bro, he does it for the love. What was the statement? He does it for the—'I do it for the benefit of humanity.' I think he got healthcare. In OpenAI's defense, they do need to raise a lot of money and they need to incentivize their employees, but that doesn't mean they need to take over the whole thing. The nonprofit portion can still stay the nonprofit portion, get the lion's share of the benefits, and be the board. Then he can have an incentive package, and employees can have an incentive package. Why don't they get a percentage of the revenue? I don't understand. It has to be bought out right now for $40 billion, and then the whole thing disappears into a closed system. That part makes no sense to me. That's called a shell game and a scam.
turns.zh: 他们还拿到了禁令。嘿，就因为他把 OpenAI 变成一个封闭的东西，简直是个滑头。我本人挺喜欢 Sam，但我认为他做的事是史上最滑头的操作，完全是为了个人利益。如果他——还有那些谎话，比如‘我没有股权，我只有医保’——不，兄弟，他做这事是为了爱。他怎么说来着？他做这事是为了——‘我为了人类利益而做’。我觉得他拿到了医保。为 OpenAI 辩护的话，他们确实需要筹集大量资金，也需要激励员工，但这不意味着他们需要接管整个公司。非营利部分可以继续保持非营利，获得大部分利益，并担任董事会。然后他可以有一个激励方案，员工也可以有。为什么他们不拿收入的一定比例？我不明白。现在必须用 400 亿美元买断，然后整个东西就消失在一个封闭系统里。这部分对我来说完全说不通。那叫骗局和欺诈。

turns.en: Yeah, I think Sam and his team would do better to leave the nonprofit part alone, leave an actual independent nonprofit board in charge, and then have a strong incentive plan and a strong fundraising plan for the investors and the employees. So I think this is workable. It's just trying to grab it all seems way off, especially when it was built on open algorithms from Google, open data from the web, and nonprofit funding from Elon and others.
turns.zh: 是的，我认为 Sam 和他的团队最好保持非营利部分不变，让一个真正独立的非营利董事会负责，然后为投资者和员工制定一个强有力的激励计划和筹款计划。所以我认为这是可行的。试图全部攫取似乎太过分了，尤其是它建立在 Google 的开放算法、网络上的开放数据以及 Elon 和其他人的非营利资助之上。

turns.en: I mean, what a great proposal we just workshopped here. What if they just—they make six billion a year, just take 10% of it, 600 million every year, and that goes into a bonus? They're losing money, Jason, so they have to—okay, eventually, no, but even equity—they could give equity to the people building it, but they could still leave it in the control of the nonprofit. I just don't understand this conversion. I mean, there was a board coup, right? The board tried to fire him. Now it's his pettiness, right? And yeah, they'll get an independent valuation, but we all know that game: you hire a valuation expert who's going to say what you want them to say, and they'll check a box. If you're going to capture the lifecycle of all future value or build superintelligence that's worth a lot more—that's why Elon just spent $100 billion. Exactly. You're saying the things that the regulators and the legal community have no insight into, because they'll see a fairness opinion and think, 'Oh, it says fairness and opinion, two words side by side, it must be fair,' and they don't know how all of this stuff is gamed.

It makes me want to do something useful with my life. You know, Elon always makes me question, 'Am I doing something useful enough with my life?' It's why I don't want to be an investor. You know, Peter Thiel, ironically he's an investor, but he's inspirational that way too, because he's like, 'Yeah, the future doesn't just happen. You have to go make it.' So you know, we get to go make the future. And I'm just glad that Elon and DOGE and others are making the future.
turns.zh: 我想和许多人一样，我曾经相信'历史的大趋势'这种心态，就是觉得：'好吧，这是不可避免的，事情就是这样。政府总是越来越大，越来越慢，我们只能在他们关闭一切、我们变成欧洲之前努力把事情建成。' 但后来发生的事情是，你知道，凯撒越过了卢比孔河。'历史伟人理论'应验了，我们就生活在那个时代。这对我们所有人都是激励。尽管山姆·奥特曼和埃隆目前有争执，我知道山姆曾经受到埃隆的启发，我想我们所有人都受到埃隆的启发。我的意思是，这个人可以一边玩暗黑破坏神，一边管理 DOGE，同时运营 SpaceX、特斯拉、Boring 和 Neuralink。这简直令人难以置信。这让我们——这就是为什么我现在在做一家硬件公司。这让我想在有生之年做些有用的事。你知道，埃隆总是让我质疑：'我这辈子做的事情足够有用吗？' 这就是为什么我不想做投资人。你知道，彼得·蒂尔，讽刺的是他是个投资人，但他在那方面也很鼓舞人心，因为他会说：'没错，未来不会自己发生。你必须去创造它。' 所以你知道，我们可以去创造未来。我很高兴埃隆、DOGE 和其他人正在创造未来。
